Only 16 blocks (N=4096, N_TILE=256) → 16 blocks on 114 SMs → severe underutilization. 2. Launch overhead dominates (each launch ~5-10µs, but 107µs for one... actually the kernel itself is slow). Wait, 107µs for 5MB? At 47GB/s that's way below H100's 2TB/s. The problem: only 16 blocks → not enough parallelism to saturate bandwidth, AND each thread does serial work. Let me reconsider. Actually 16 blocks × 256 threads = 4096 threads. Each thread reads 1152 bytes (the weight column for its output). The reads are coalesced within a block (256 consecutive bytes per kby-row). But with only 16 blocks, the memory subsystem isn't saturated. Also, the per-thread loop is long (1152 iterations of dependent-ish work), so latency isn't hidden well. The 107µs is mostly launch overhead + low parallelism. For a real GEMV in the megakernel (no launch overhead, fused), I need better parallelism. Key issue: for batch-1 GEMV with N=4096, to saturate bandwidth I should use split-K or assign more threads per output. Let me reconsider: with 114 SMs, I want ~hundreds of blocks. For N=4096, that means smaller N_TILE or split-K. Let me reconsider the access pattern for better bandwidth. The issue is N=4096 only gives 16 tiles of 256. To get 114+ blocks, I need either N_TILE~32 (128 tiles) or split-K. Split-K: divide K into chunks, each (n-tile, k-chunk) is a block, partial sums atomically reduced. For N=4096, K=2304: with N_TILE=256, k-chunks of 128 (one group) → 16 n-tiles × 18 groups = 288 blocks. Each block reads its n-tile × 128 k-values. Atomic-add partial to y[n]. 288 blocks → good parallelism. But atomic contention: 288 blocks writing to 4096 outputs, each output gets 18 atomic adds → moderate contention. Hmm. Alternative: smaller N_TILE. N_TILE=32 → for N=4096, 128 blocks. Each block 32 threads?? That wastes threads (block has 256 threads, only 32 used). Bad. Use blockDim=32 (one warp) per block. Then 128 blocks of 32 threads. Each block does N_TILE=32 outputs, 32 threads. Loop over K. This gives 128 blocks → better than 16. But 32-thread blocks underutilize warp-level... actually 32 threads = 1 warp, fine. 128 blocks × 1 warp. Each warp reads 32 consecutive bytes per kby-row (one transaction). For K=2304, 1152 byte-reads per warp. 128 warps. Total threads 4096 (same as before!). So same parallelism. Hmm, the number of THREADS is what matters for bandwidth, not blocks. 4096 threads regardless. The real issue: 4096 threads is too few to saturate H100 bandwidth. H100 wants ~tens of thousands of threads (or many outstanding loads per thread) to hide HBM latency (~400 cycles). So I need MORE threads. Options: 1. Larger N_TILE (more threads per block) — but then fewer blocks, and threads still total N. For N=4096, max threads = 4096 (one per output). Can't exceed N without split-K. 2. Split-K: each output computed by multiple threads (split the K dot), then reduce. This multiplies threads by split factor. E.g., split-K=8 → 4096×8 = 32K threads. Each thread does K/8=288 of the dot, then reduce across the 8 threads (via warp/block reduce + atomic). This gives 8× more threads → better bandwidth. So for batch-1 GEMV, split-K is essential to get enough threads. Let me redesign with split-K. Split-K GEMV design: - Grid: blocks indexed by (n-tile, k-chunk). - Each block: N_TILE outputs, K_CHUNK k-values. Computes partial dot for its outputs over its k-chunk. - Reduce: partial sums across k-chunks for each output. Use atomic add to y[n], OR a two-pass reduce. Atomic add: simple but contention. For N=4096 with split into S k-chunks, each y[n] gets S atomic adds. If S=8, 8 atomics per output, 4096 outputs → 32K atomics. Contention is per-output (8 adds spread in time) → OK-ish. fp32 atomic add is supported on H100. Accuracy: atomic add order is nondeterministic → slightly different from reference, but cosine 0.98 is lenient. Should be fine. Alternatively, use a reduction buffer: partial[S, N], then a separate reduce kernel. But that's a 2nd kernel (or 2nd phase in megakernel). In the megakernel, I can have a phase that writes partial[S,N] then... no, I'd need to reduce. Atomic is simpler (1 phase). Hmm, but actually — in the MEGAKERNEL, this GEMV is one PHASE among many, and ALL blocks participate in each phase (persistent). So for the q_proj phase (N=4096), I have G blocks (say 456). I assign them to (n-tile, k-chunk) tiles. With N_TILE and K-split chosen so num_tiles ≈ G. E.g., N_TILE=8, K-split such that 4096/8 × K-split = G. 512 × K-split = 456 → K-split ~1. Hmm. Or N_TILE=32: 128 n-tiles × K-split. For G=456, K-split=4 → 512 tiles (close). Wait, I'm overcomplicating. In the persistent megakernel, each block loops over a SET of tiles (assigned round-robin). So I don't need num_tiles == G. I need num_tiles large enough to give good parallelism, and each block does num_tiles/G tiles. For bandwidth, I want enough total threads = G × blockDim. With G=456, blockDim=256 → 116K threads. That's plenty! The issue in my STANDALONE test was only 16 blocks (because I launched grid=16). In the megakernel with G=456 blocks ALL doing the q_proj phase, even if each handles a tiny tile, total threads = 116K → saturates bandwidth. So the fix: in the megakernel, ALL G blocks participate in each GEMV phase, each handling a slice. For q_proj (N=4096), split N across G blocks: each block handles N/G ≈ 9 outputs (with 256 threads, but only ~9 outputs → 9 threads active, rest idle). That's bad (247 idle threads/block). Hmm. So the assignment matters. Let me think: for a GEMV with N outputs and G blocks, B threads each: - If I assign 1 output per thread, I need N threads total. With G×B threads available, if G×B > N, some threads idle. For N=4096, G×B=116K → only 4096 threads work, 112K idle. The 4096 working threads read the weight (5MB) but slowly (not enough threads). - To use more threads, split-K: assign (output, k-chunk) per thread. With S k-chunks, N×S threads work. For N=4096, S=28 → 114K threads (≈ G×B). Each thread does K/28 ≈ 82 of the dot. Then reduce across S threads per output. So split-K is needed to engage all threads. Let me design the persistent GEMV with split-K: - For a GEMV of size N (outputs), K (reduction): - Choose S = number of k-chunks. Each block handles a set of (output-tile, k-chunk) work items. - Actually, let me make each BLOCK handle one output-tile of width W outputs, over ONE k-chunk. Threads within block: W threads do the dot (each one output, over the k-chunk), rest help? Let me simplify with a clean per-output split-K: - Total work units = N outputs. Each output's dot (length K) is split among S threads (S = split factor). - Block has B threads. A block handles B/S outputs (each output uses S threads). E.g., B=256, S=8 → block handles 32 outputs, using 8 threads each (32×8=256). - Grid: num_blocks = ceil(N / 32). For N=4096 → 128 blocks. Each block: 32 outputs × 8 threads/output. The 8 threads split the K dot (each does K/8), then warp-reduce (the 8 threads are part of warps... need careful reduction). Reduction across 8 threads per output: if the 8 threads for one output are in the same warp, use warp shuffles. 32 outputs × 8 threads = 256 = 8 warps. Each warp (32 threads) handles 4 outputs (4×8=32). Within a warp, 4 groups of 8 threads, each group does one output. Warp shuffle reduce within each group of 8 (using `__shfl_xor` masks). Tricky but doable. Alternatively, simpler: 1 output per warp (32 threads split the K dot). Block = 8 warps → 8 outputs per block. For N=4096 → 512 blocks. Each warp: 32 threads split K=2304 → 72 k-values/thread, warp-reduce to 1 output. 512 blocks × 8 warps = 4096 warps = 131K threads. Good parallelism! And reduction is a clean warp-shuffle (32 threads → 1). So: **1 warp per output, 32 threads split-K the dot, warp-reduce.** Block of 8 warps (256 threads) handles 8 outputs. Grid = N/8 blocks (for the tiles), but in persistent megakernel, G blocks each handle multiple output-tiles. For the persistent kernel: G blocks. Each block, in a GEMV phase, loops over output-tiles assigned round-robin (tile = 8 outputs). Block b handles tiles {b, b+G, ...}. For N=4096, 512 tiles, G=456 → block 0 handles ~2 tiles, others 1. Mostly 1 tile/block → 456 blocks × 8 outputs = 3648 outputs (but N=4096) → some blocks do 2. Each block reads 8 outputs × full K weight = 8 × 2304 × 0.5 = 9.2KB. ×456 blocks = 4.2MB ≈ the weight. Good. Total threads working = 456×256 = 116K. For coalescing: within a warp (32 threads, 1 output), the 32 threads split K. Reading the weight: thread t in warp reads W[k, n] for k = t, t+32, ... For FIXED n (one output), varying k → column read (strided by N)! Uncoalesced. BAD again. Ugh, the coalescing problem returns with split-K-per-output. Because each warp works on ONE output (one column of W), reading down the column = strided. So there's a tension: - "Block handles N_TILE consecutive outputs, threads coalesce on N" → coalesced reads, but few outputs per block → few blocks (N/N_TILE). - "Warp per output, split-K" → many warps (good parallelism) but strided column reads (bad coalescing). The resolution: the weight must be laid out so that ONE output's data is contiguous (out-major), THEN warp-per-output reads contiguously. That's the transpose I considered earlier! So: transpose weight to out-major [N, K//2] (and scales [N, ng]). Then warp-per-output: warp handles output n, reads W_out[n, :] = contiguous row of K//2 bytes. 32 threads split the K//2 bytes: thread t reads bytes t, t+32, ... (every 32nd byte of the row). That's strided within the row by 32 bytes... but it's within a contiguous row, and 32 threads reading every 32 bytes = coalesced (32 threads × 4-byte stride... actually 1-byte stride per thread spaced 32 → addresses n*K2 + t, n*K2+t+32, ... → for the 32 threads at once: addresses n*K2+0, n*K2+32, n*K2+64,... → stride 32 between consecutive threads → NOT coalesced into one transaction (32-byte stride means threads 0 and 1 are 32 apart → separate 32B sectors). Hmm, that's 32 separate 1-byte reads → terrible. Wait, I need to think about coalescing more carefully. For coalescing, consecutive threads should read consecutive addresses. If 32 threads read every 32nd byte (stride 32), consecutive threads are 32 bytes apart → each in a different 32B sector → 32 sectors → 32 transactions. Bad. So for warp-per-output reading a contiguous row, I want consecutive threads to read consecutive bytes. But then they'd be reading the SAME output's consecutive k-bytes, which means they're NOT splitting K (they're reading adjacent k's, then need to combine). Actually: thread t reads byte t of the row (k=2t, 2t+1). Then the 32 threads collectively have bytes 0..31 = k=0..63. To compute the dot, I need to SUM x[k]*w[k] over all k — but each thread has only 2 k's. So thread t has partial = x[2t]*w0 + x[2t+1]*w1. Then warp-reduce (sum across 32 threads) → dot over k=0..63. But that's only 64 k's (one chunk)! The full K=2304 needs K/64 = 36 chunks. So the warp iterates over 36 chunks, each chunk: 32 threads read 32 consecutive bytes (coalesced!), compute partial, accumulate locally, after all chunks warp-reduce. YES. This works and is coalesced: warp iterates over byte-chunks of 32 bytes (64 k-values). Each chunk: 32 threads read 32 consecutive bytes of the row (one 32B transaction, coalesced). Accumulate partial in each thread. After K/64 chunks, warp-reduce sum → the dot for output n. So with out-major layout [N, K//2], warp-per-output, chunked coalesced reads → efficient. Each warp produces one output. Block of 8 warps = 8 outputs. Let me reconsider the scales: [N, ng] out-major. For output n, ng scale values. The warp loads scale[g] (broadcast) at the start of group g. So I WILL transpose the weights to out-major. As discussed, I keep registered buffers in reference layout (for load_state_dict) and create transposed working buffers lazily. The transpose is a one-time setup. Actually wait — can I avoid the explicit transpose and just have the kernel read the in-major layout cleverly? The in-major layout [K//2, N]: warp-per-output reads column = strided. The "block handles N_TILE consecutive, threads coalesce on N" reads rows (coalesced) but gives few blocks. Neither is great for batch-1. The out-major transpose is the clean solution. Let me just do the transpose. Transposing 90 buffers once is cheap (setup). Let me do it. Hold on, let me reconsider — is there an even simpler high-performance option? What about the "block handles N_TILE consecutive outputs, threads = N_TILE, each thread one output, loop over K" pattern, but with MANY blocks via split over... no, that's the few-blocks problem. Actually, the cleanest high-performance batch-1 W4A16 GEMV is exactly: transpose to out-major, one warp (or few warps) per output, coalesced streaming of the weight row, warp-reduce. This is the standard. Let me implement it with the transpose. Actually, even better for utilizing threads: instead of 1 warp per output, use a whole BLOCK per output but that's wasteful for small K. Or 1 warp per output is standard. Let me also consider: for SMALL N (like 576, 1024), I have few outputs → few warps → underutilization. But those are small weights (0.6-1.2MB), so even at low utilization they're fast. The big weights (q_proj 4.7MB, expert weights) have N=1024-4096 → enough warps. Hmm wait, for N=1024, that's 1024 warps = 256 blocks (8 warps/block). G=456 → ~1 block per... no, 256 blocks < 456, so 256 blocks work, 200 idle. 256 blocks × 8 warps = 2048 warps = 65K threads. Reads 1024 outputs × K weight. For gate weight [2304,1024] = 1.18MB. 65K threads → should saturate. Fine. Let me reconsider the persistent assignment. In the megakernel, G blocks. For a GEMV phase with N outputs, each output = 1 warp = 1/8 of a block. So work units = N warps. Assign warps to blocks: total warps = G×8. If N < G×8, some warps idle. Block b handles output-warps {b*8..b*8+7} and wraps: warp w (global) → output w, if w<N. Plus, if N > G×8, each warp handles multiple outputs (loop). For N=4096, G×8 = 3648 < 4096 → some warps do 2 outputs (loop). Fine. So the GEMV phase: each block's 8 warps, each warp computes output(s) via coalesced streaming + warp reduce. Let me now also reconsider: do I even need the full transpose, or can I store the weight already in a warp-friendly layout? The transpose to [N, K//2] makes output n's data contiguous. But the within-row access is chunks of 32 bytes (coalesced). Good. Let me transpose. Now, about the scales/zeros: reference shape [ng, N]. Transpose to [N, ng]. For output n, the ng scales are contiguous (a row). Warp loads them as needed. Or even simpler: since ng is small (K/128 = 18 for K=2304, 4 for K=512), the warp can load all ng scales into registers at the start. Let me now also handle the EXPERT weights. QuantExperts.w_q is [E, K//2, N]. For expert e, output n: weight is w_q[e, :, n]. Transpose to [E, N, K//2] (per expert out-major). Then warp-per-(expert, output). For the MoE phase with 9 active experts × N outputs each = 9N warps. For down-proj N=2304 → 9×2304 = 20736 warps = 2592 blocks. G=456 → each block's 8 warps handle ~20736/3648 ≈ 5.7 outputs. Loop. Good parallelism, big traffic (the point). OK the design is solid. Let me also reconsider whether to transpose scales/zeros or keep [ng,N] and gather. For the GEMV with out-major weight, accessing scale[g, n] for fixed n, varying g: if scales are [ng, N], scale[g,n] is strided (stride N). For ng=18, that's 18 strided reads — minor (small). But cleaner to transpose to [N,ng] so scale[n, :] contiguous. Let me transpose scales/zeros too. Cheap. Alright. Let me now write a revised standalone dequant-GEMV test with the out-major transpose + warp-per-output + coalesced streaming, and measure bandwidth. This validates the high-performance core. Let me write it. Design of warp-per-output kernel: ``` __global__ void dq_gemv_warp(uint8_t* WqT, // [N, K//2] out-major bf16* ScT, // [N, ng] bf16* ZrT, // [N, ng] bf16* x, // [K] float* y, // [N] int K, int N) { int warp = (blockIdx.x * blockDim.x + threadIdx.x) / 32; // global warp id int lane = threadIdx.x % 32; if (warp >= N) return; int K2 = K/2; int ng = K/128; uint8_t* row = WqT + warp * K2; // output warp's weight row bf16* sc = ScT + warp * ng; bf16* zr = ZrT + warp * ng; float acc = 0.0f; // iterate over groups of 128 k = 64 bytes for (int g = 0; g < ng; g++) { float s = bf162f(sc[g]); float z = bf162f(zr[g]); // 64 bytes in this group, processed in 2 chunks of 32 bytes (64 k-values each) // chunk c: bytes [g*64 + c*32, ... +32], k = 2*byteindex #pragma unroll for (int c = 0; c < 2; c++) { int bi = g*64 + c*32 + lane; // byte index, lane reads consecutive -> coalesced uint8_t byte = row[bi]; int k0 = 2*bi; float w0 = ((float)(byte&0xF) - z) * s; float w1 = ((float)((byte>>4)&0xF) - z) * s; acc += bf162f(x[k0]) * w0 + bf162f(x[k0+1]) * w1; } } // warp reduce for (int off=16; off>0; off/=2) acc += __shfl_xor_sync(-1, acc, off); if (lane==0) y[warp] = acc; } ``` Wait, the chunk size: group = 128 k-values = 64 bytes. 32 threads read 32 bytes per chunk → 2 chunks per group. Each chunk: lane reads byte bi=g*64+c*32+lane (consecutive lanes → consecutive bytes → coalesced 32-byte read). Each byte → 2 k-values (k0, k0+1). So per chunk, 64 k-values. 2 chunks = 128 k-values = 1 group. x[k0]: all lanes in the warp read x at different k0 (k0 = 2*bi varies by lane). So 32 different x elements → 32 reads from global (x). x is bf16. If x is in shared mem, 32 different addresses → could have bank conflicts but broadcast-ish. Better load x to shared mem once. But x[k0] for k0=2*bi, bi = g*64+c*32+lane → k0 = 2*(g*64+c*32+lane) = 128g + 64c + 2*lane. For c=0: k0 = 128g + 2*lane (even, spaced 2). For c=1: k0 = 128g+64+2*lane. So within a chunk, lanes read x[128g+2*lane] and x[128g+2*lane+1]. These are 32 pairs of consecutive bf16 → effectively reading x[128g .. 128g+63] (64 consecutive bf16). 32 lanes reading 2 consecutive each, covering 64 elements. Coalesced if x in global (64 bf16 = 128 bytes, 32 lanes × 2 reads). But better in shared. Let me load x[K] to shared mem (cooperative) at block start, then read from shared. Actually, x is read repeatedly (every output reads all of x). So x MUST be in shared mem (or L1 cache). Loading x[K] to shared once per block is right. K=2304 bf16 = 4.6KB. As fp32 in shared = 9.2KB. Fine. Hmm wait, but different warps in a block read x at different k's — but it's the same x. Shared mem broadcast: if all warps read the same x[k], broadcast. But they read different k's (each warp's output reads all k). Within a warp, the 32 lanes read 64 different x elements (different addresses) → 64 shared mem reads, possible bank conflicts (bf16 → pack into fp32?). Let me store x as fp32 in shared (one per k), lanes read x_s[k0] = different k0 → different banks mostly. Should be OK. Actually, to reduce shared mem traffic, I can have each lane keep its x values in registers. But the lane reads x[k0] for k0 = 128g+64c+2*lane across g,c. Over the loop, lane reads x[2*lane], x[64+2*lane], x[128+2*lane], ... i.e., x at stride 128, offset 2*lane (and +1). So each lane touches K/128 × 2 = 36 x-values. Could pre-load into registers? Complex. Let me just read from shared mem; H100 shared mem is fast and bank conflicts minimal. Let me write this and measure. I expect much better bandwidth (hundreds of GB/s to ~1TB/s). Let me write the test with transpose + warp kernel. Actually, I realize for the standalone test I should also compare against reference to ensure the transpose + warp reduce is correct. Let me do correctness + bandwidth. Let me write scratch/test_gemv2.py. I'll structure: build W [K,N], quantize, transpose to out-major, run warp kernel, compare. For transpose: Wq is [K//2, N] → WqT [N, K//2] = Wq.t().contiguous(). Sc [ng,N] → [N,ng]. Same. Let me write it.

Dequant-GEMV is correct (cos=1.0), but only 47 GB/s — only 16 blocks, far too few threads. Batch-1 GEMV needs split-K + coalesced reads. The fix: transpose weights to out-major [N, K//2], one warp per output, coalesced streaming of the weight row, warp-reduce. Let me validate that.

The issue now: launch overhead (~5-10µs per launch) dominates these tiny kernels. For 5MB at 328 GB/s = 15µs, but launch overhead is ~5µs of that. In the megakernel (fused, no per-op launch), the actual compute will be faster. But the bandwidth is still low — I want closer to 1-2 TB/s. Wait, the issue: these standalone launches have launch overhead. The 15µs includes ~5µs launch. So actual kernel ~10µs → 500 GB/s. Still low. The problem: not enough warps/occupancy for small N, and the warp-per-output with only N warps. For N=4096: 4096 warps = 512 blocks (8 warps/block). 512 blocks on 114 SMs = ~4.5 blocks/SM. Each block 256 threads = 4.5×256 = 1152 threads/SM. H100 SM has 2048 max threads → ~56% occupancy. Should be OK but the bandwidth is 328 GB/s. Hmm, maybe the issue is the x shared mem load or the access pattern. Let me think. Actually 328 GB/s for a memory-bound kernel reading 5MB... Let me reconsider. Actually wait — the bandwidth includes only the weight. Each warp reads K/2 = 1152 bytes of weight + ng×2 scale bytes. For 4096 warps, total weight read = 4096 × 1152 = 4.7MB (the full weight). Good, each weight byte read once. At 328 GB/s, 4.7MB takes 14µs. To hit 2TB/s would be 2.4µs. The gap: likely memory latency not hidden + the x shared-mem reload per block (each block reloads x[K] from global to shared — that's K bf16 = 4.6KB per block × 512 blocks = 2.3MB of x reads, minor). The main weight read is coalesced (32-byte chunks). Maybe the warp reduction or the loop overhead. Let me not over-optimize the standalone — in the megakernel, the launch overhead is gone and phases are fused. The key question is whether the GEMV achieves good bandwidth when run as a phase. Let me not obsess; 328 GB/s standalone (with launch overhead) suggests maybe 500+ GB/s in-kernel. For the full model (~226MB weight traffic), at 500 GB/s = 450µs; at 1TB/s = 226µs. Hmm, 450µs would still beat baseline (11ms) by 24x. But I'd like better. Let me think about whether the megakernel structure (all blocks doing each phase) helps. In the megakernel, ALL G blocks participate in each GEMV phase. For the q_proj phase (N=4096), if G=456 blocks all participate, and each block has 8 warps, that's 3648 warps for 4096 outputs → ~1 warp per output (some do 2). Same as standalone basically. So bandwidth similar (~328-500 GB/s). To improve bandwidth, I could: 1. Increase occupancy / outstanding loads (more warps per output, i.e., split the K further with more threads per output). But warp-per-output already uses 32 threads/output. 2. Use larger vectorized loads (read 4 bytes = uint32 at once). Currently each lane reads 1 byte. Reading 4 bytes/lane would process 8 k-values/lane/chunk → fewer iterations, more work per load. Let me vectorize: each lane reads a uint32 (4 bytes) per chunk, processes 8 k-values. Then a "chunk" covers 32 lanes × 8 k = 256 k-values = 128 bytes. Group=128 k = 64 bytes = half a chunk. Hmm, let me realign. Actually, vectorization: read uint32 (4 bytes) → 8 int4 → 8 k-values. A group is 128 k = 64 bytes = 16 uint32s. 32 lanes reading uint32: each lane reads 1 uint32 = 4 bytes. 32 lanes × 4 bytes = 128 bytes per chunk = 256 k-values = 2 groups. So one chunk of 32 uint32 loads covers 2 groups (256 k). Then I load 2 scales (one per group). This halves the number of load instructions and processes more per load. Should improve bandwidth. Let me vectorize with uint32 reads. But this is optimization — let me first get the megakernel WORKING and CORRECT, then optimize the GEMV. The correctness is validated. For the buffer registration, I MUST match reference exactly: - QuantLinear: w_q [in//2, out] uint8, scales [in//128, out] bf16, zeros [in//128, out] bf16. - QuantExperts: w_q [n, in//2, out] uint8, scales [n, in//128, out] bf16, zeros [n, in//128, out] bf16. - KDA: q_proj,k_proj,v_proj,g_proj QuantLinear(d, H*Dk); beta_proj nn.Linear(d, H, bias=False, bf16); conv_w Parameter [3, H*Dk, short_conv] bf16; o_proj QuantLinear(H*Dk, d). - MLA: q_proj QuantLinear(d, H*(qk_nope+qk_rope)); kv_a QuantLinear(d, kv_lora+qk_rope); kv_b QuantLinear(kv_lora, H*(qk_nope+v_head)); o_proj QuantLinear(H*v_head, d). - MoE: router nn.Linear(d, E, bf16); gate/up/down QuantExperts(E, d, m); s_gate/s_up/s_down QuantExperts(n_shared, d, m) and (n_shared, m, d). - Block: attn_norm, moe_norm Parameters [hidden] bf16; attn (KDA/MLA); moe. - Model: blocks ModuleList. I'll copy these from reference.py (adapted). The load_state_dict will fill them. Good. Now the megakernel. Let me design the full thing. Given complexity, I'll build the CUDA source as a big string. Let me lay out the phases and the device functions. Let me define the model constants (H=32, Dk=128, hidden=2304, etc.) as #defines. Let me reconsider the state representation passed to the kernel. I'll pass state tensor pointers to the kernel. For KDA: S_ptr, cq_ptr, ck_ptr, cv_ptr (per block). For MLA: c_kv_buf ptr, k_rope_buf ptr, L (current length). The hidden vector threads through blocks. I'll use scratch buffers for intermediate activations. Let me enumerate ALL scratch buffers needed (global memory): - Per block, the normed hidden isn't materialized (folded into GEMV). But I need sumsq scalars: sumsq_attn[block] (for attn norm), sumsq_moe[block] (for moe norm). Actually computed inline. I'll have a small sumsq buffer [num_blocks*2] or compute per block. - xn is folded (not stored). - KDA: q,k,v (conv'd) [4096] each — needed for recurrent. g [4096], beta [32]. o [4096] (recurrent output). a = o_proj output (hidden, but folded with residual). - MLA: q_nope[32,128], q_rope[32,64] (rope applied), c_kv_new[512], k_rope_new[64], q_absorb[512,32], scores[L+1,32], p[L+1,32], v_accum[32,512], o[32,128]. - MoE: router_scores[64], idx[8] (or compute redundantly), w[8], hh[9,1024], moe_out[2304]. - hidden buffers: x_buf [2304] (current hidden), and a couple scratch. Let me write them as device functions operating on the scratch. Each device function takes (args struct, block_id, grid_size, shared_mem). Let me define an Args struct holding all pointers + sizes. I'll pass it by value (it's large but < 4KB). Let me count Args pointers: - weights: 19 QuantLinear × 3 = 57, + 6 QuantExperts × 3 = 18, + bf16 params (beta_proj×3, conv_w×3, router×4, attn_norm×4, moe_norm×4 = 18). Total ~93 pointers. - state: KDA S×3, cq×3, ck×3, cv×3 = 12; MLA c_kv, k_rope = 2. Total 14. - scratch: x_buf, scores, p, q,k,v,g (kda), o, q_nope,q_rope,q_absorb,v_accum (mla), hh, moe_out, router_scores, sumsq, etc. ~20. - input hidden, output hidden: 2. Total ~130 pointers × 8 bytes = 1040 bytes. Plus ints (L, n_active, etc.). Under 4KB. OK pass by value as a struct. Hmm, building a 130-field struct is tedious but mechanical. Let me instead pass pointers via a device array (int64) + use indices. Actually, passing a struct by value is cleaner for the kernel code (named fields). But 130 fields... Alternative: Pass a single int64* `ptrs` array (device memory) holding all 130 pointers in a documented order, with #define indices. The kernel reads ptrs[IDX] when needed. The Args struct is just {int64* ptrs; int* ints; ...} — few fields. The ptrs array is built in Python (once, cached) and passed as a device tensor. But the pointer VALUES must be refreshed when state/scratch changes... Ugh, the state pointers change per _run (benchmark recreates state). So I can't fully cache ptrs. Let me reconsider. Within a single step() call, all pointers are known (weights from self, state from arg, scratch from self). I build the ptrs array fresh each step in Python (a list of int64 pointer values), copy to a device tensor (small, ~1KB), pass to kernel. The copy is a cudaMemcpyAsync (~1KB) — not a kernel launch. Is that acceptable? It's metadata transfer, like passing kernel args. I think it's fine (not a "kernel launch", not a "per-op loop"). But to be safe re: the "single kernel launch" rule, let me minimize. Actually — the cleanest and safest: pass the pointers as actual kernel arguments via the C++ launcher. The C++ function takes a `std::vector<at::Tensor>` (or many Tensor args), extracts data_ptr(), packs into a struct, launches. The struct goes in kernel constant memory (kernel args). No device array, no extra copy. The packing is on CPU (fast). This is the idiomatic torch extension way. To avoid writing a 130-arg C++ signature, I use `std::vector<at::Tensor>` as ONE arg. Python passes a list of tensors → becomes std::vector<at::Tensor>. C++ iterates, gets data_ptr(), fills struct. Let me do that. The C++ launcher: ```cpp struct Args { /* 130 pointers + ints */ }; void launch(std::vector<at::Tensor> ts, int64_t L, int gen, ...) { Args a; a.q_proj_wq = ts[0].data_ptr<uint8_t>(); ... mega<<<G, B, smem, stream>>>(a, L, gen); } ``` Filling 130 fields manually is tedious. Let me instead keep the pointers in a device array but build it efficiently. Hmm. Actually, simplest robust approach: **flatten all weights into a few big contiguous buffers at setup, pass ~6 base pointers + offsets as kernel args (offsets are #define constants since dims are fixed).** The state and scratch pointers (which change) are passed as the few that vary (~14 state + ~6 scratch = 20 pointers). So total kernel args ≈ 6 (weight bases) + 20 (state/scratch) + scalars = ~30 args. Very manageable, and only the ~20 state/scratch pointers are "dynamic". But flattening weights requires computing offsets in Python AND matching #defines in CUDA. The dims are FIXED (hidden=2304, etc.), so offsets are fixed. I'll define them once. Risk: mismatch. Mitigation: I'll verify by reading back a few weights after flattening and comparing to originals in Python (assert). If offsets are right, proceed. Hmm, this is a lot of careful work. Let me reconsider the std::vector<at::Tensor> approach but auto-generate the struct fill. Actually, I can avoid a struct: pass the vector's data_ptrs directly. But the kernel needs them accessible. OK let me just go with: **flat weight buffers (one per category) + #define offsets + dynamic state/scratch pointers as explicit args.** I'll write a Python helper that, given the model, builds the flat buffers and returns them, and writes the offset #defines. To avoid manual error, I'll compute offsets in Python with a clear layout function and EMIT the #defines as a formatted string into the CUDA source at load time (f-string). That way the offsets in CUDA come from the SAME Python computation → guaranteed consistent! Yes! I'll generate the CUDA source with f-string interpolation for the offset #defines, computed in Python. So Python computes `OFF_QPROJ_WQ = ...` and injects `#define OFF_QPROJ_WQ {value}` into the source. Single source of truth. No mismatch. This is clean. Let me do this: - Python: define the weight layout (ordered list of weights with sizes), compute offsets, build flat buffers (WQ uint8, SC bf16, ZS bf16, BF bf16), inject #defines into CUDA source. - CUDA: `WQ[OFF_...]` accesses. - Dynamic args: state ptrs + scratch ptrs + scalars, passed as explicit kernel args. The flat buffers are stored on self (persistent). The offsets are known at module load (Python computes them when building the source, which happens at import). Hmm, but the source is built at import (when solution.py loads), before any model exists. The offsets depend only on fixed dims (known at import). So I compute offsets at import, inject into source, compile. Then when a model is created and step() called (first time), I build the flat buffers from the (now-populated) weight buffers. Good. Let me design the flat layout. Categories: 1. WQ (uint8): all packed int4 weights. Order: for each block, for each QuantLinear proj, w_q flattened [K//2 * N]; for each QuantExperts, w_q [E, K//2, N] flattened. 2. SC (bf16): all scales, same order, [ng*N] or [E*ng*N]. 3. ZS (bf16): all zeros. 4. BF (bf16): all bf16 params (beta_proj, conv_w, router, norms). Actually, for the kernel, I'll TRANSPOSE each weight to out-major before flattening (for coalesced warp-per-output access). So in Python setup, for each QuantLinear: WqT = w_q.t().contiguous() ([N, K//2]), ScT = scales.t().contiguous() ([N,ng]), ZrT = zeros.t().contiguous(). Flatten these transposed into the WQ/SC/ZS buffers. The offsets index into these flattened transposed buffers. For QuantExperts [E, K//2, N]: transpose per-expert to [E, N, K//2] → `w_q.transpose(1,2).contiguous()`. Scales [E, ng, N] → [E, N, ng] → `.transpose(1,2).contiguous()`. OK. Let me now also reconsider: do I really need out-major for ALL weights, or can some stay in-major? The MoE expert weights: warp-per-(expert,output) reads expert e's output n row. With [E,N,K//2] layout, row (e,n,:) is contiguous. Good. So transpose experts too. Alright. Let me also handle the bf16 params (beta_proj, conv_w, router, norms) — small, just flatten into BF with offsets. This is a big build. Let me start writing solution.py incrementally. I'll begin with the module structure (mirror reference) + a WORKING but slow step (using torch ops, like baseline) to validate load_state_dict works and get a correctness baseline. Then replace step with the megakernel. Wait — but a torch-based step would be "per-op kernel loop" which fails the authenticity judge. But for MY iterative development, I want a correct reference to test against. I already HAVE reference.py for that. So I don't need a torch step in solution.py. I'll go straight to the megakernel, testing against reference.py via check.py. Actually, a staged approach is safer: - Stage 1: solution.py with full module structure + step that does the forward in PURE TORCH (reimplemented, not importing reference/baseline). This passes correctness (cosine ~1.0) but fails the megakernel judge (it's eager). I use it to validate load_state_dict + correctness of my reimplementation. - Stage 2: replace step with megakernel, validating against the torch version AND reference. But stage 1's torch step, if it accidentally becomes the final, fails the judge. I'll make sure to replace it. Stage 1 helps me debug the math separately from the kernel. Given the kernel is complex, having a torch reference of MY OWN (matching reference) is useful. But reference.py IS the reference. So I can just test the kernel against reference directly. Let me skip stage 1 and go to the kernel, using reference.py as oracle, with intermediate-buffer-dump debugging. Hmm, but debugging a 600-line megakernel by comparing only final output to reference is hard (one bug anywhere → wrong output, hard to localize). Better: build the megakernel incrementally, dumping intermediate buffers after each phase and comparing to a Python/torch computation of the same intermediate (using reference's weights). Let me create a DEBUG harness: a script that runs ONE block's phases in the kernel, dumping intermediates, and compares to a torch reference computation. I'll build phase-by-phase. Given the scope, let me be systematic. Let me first write the full module structure in solution.py + the megakernel for JUST block 0 (KDA), test it, then add MLA block, then MoE, then all 4 blocks. Actually, the megakernel must do ALL blocks in one launch. But I can build it to do block 0 first (with the other blocks as no-ops or torch), test, then extend. Since it's one kernel, I'll add phases incrementally and test the cumulative output. This is going to be a long implementation. Let me set up task tracking and start. Let me reconsider the time budget and be efficient. I'll write the CUDA kernel in one go but carefully, with debug dumps, then iterate. Let me structure the kernel code clearly. Let me define the device-function approach: - Shared memory union: I'll use a big `__shared__ float smem[SMEM/4]` reinterpreted per phase. Max usage: x vector (K=4096 max → 16KB fp32) + reduction scratch. ~16-32KB. - Per-phase device functions called from the main kernel. Let me reconsider the persistent + barrier structure once more and finalize: - Grid: G blocks (query occupancy, G = 114 * maxBlocksPerSM). Block: 256 threads (8 warps). - Each block: `bid = blockIdx.x`. - Shared by all phases: shared memory, loaded as needed. - Barrier(phase_id, gen, G): atomic-gen. - Main kernel: sequence of phases. Each phase: a device function that, based on bid, computes its tile of the phase's output, writing to scratch. Then barrier. The device functions need access to: weight base pointers, scratch pointers, dims. I'll pass a const Args& (by value) to each. Or make them global (file-scope __device__ functions taking Args). Let me define Args as a struct passed to the kernel (by value). It holds: WQ, SC, ZS, BF base ptrs (4), the dynamic state/scratch ptrs (~20), and small ints (L, etc.). The weight offsets are #defines (from Python). So Args ≈ 25 fields. Wait, but the weight offsets being #defines means the kernel "knows" the layout. And Args has the 4 base pointers. To access weight i: `WQ + OFF_W_i`. Good. Let me enumerate the dynamic pointers in Args (state + scratch that change per call or per model): State (per the 4 blocks): - block 0,1,2 (KDA): S0,S1,S2, cq0,cq1,cq2, ck0,ck1,ck2, cv0,cv1,cv2 → 12 ptrs - block 3 (MLA): c_kv_buf, k_rope_buf → 2 ptrs Scratch (per model, allocated once): - xh (current hidden, [HIDDEN]) → input/output - For KDA scratch: q,k,v,g ([C=4096] each, per active block — but 3 KDA blocks run sequentially, can reuse same scratch), o ([4096]), beta ([H]). Reuse across blocks. → q,kv,vg,o,beta = ~5 ptrs (reused per block). - For MLA scratch: q_absorb ([512*32]), scores ([Lmax*32]), p ([Lmax*32]), v_accum ([32*512]), o_mla ([32*128]), c_kv_new/k_rope_new small. → ~5 ptrs. - MoE scratch: hh ([9*1024]), router_scores ([64]), moe_out/moe_partial ([HIDDEN]). → ~3 ptrs. - sumsq buffer ([num_phases] or a few scalars), barrier counters ([num_phases]). → 2 ptrs. - output hidden ptr. So Args ≈ 12 + 2 + 5 + 5 + 3 + 2 + 1 (hidden in) = ~30 dynamic ptrs + 4 weight bases = 34 ptrs + ints. ~300 bytes. Let me now also decide: KDA blocks 0,1,2 reuse the same q/k/v/g/o scratch (since they run at different times). Yes. Similarly MoE scratch reused across the 4 MoE layers. OK let me start writing. This is a large file. Let me write it in pieces. Actually, let me reconsider the MoE routing and expert execution carefully since it's the bulk: MoE step (from reference): ``` probs = softmax(router(x)) # x = hn (normed), router [d, E] w, idx = topk(probs, 8) w = w / (w.sum()+1e-9) * routed_scaling # [8] out = sum_{j=0..7} w[j] * ffn(x, gate[idx[j]], up[idx[j]], down[idx[j]]) + ffn(x, s_gate[0], s_up[0], s_down[0]) # shared ffn(x,g,u,d) = (silu(x@g) * (x@u)) @ d ``` So 9 experts (8 routed + 1 shared). For the shared, idx is fixed (0 in s_gate etc.). I'll treat the 9 experts as a list: routed 8 (from topk) + shared 1. The expert weights: gate/up are [E, d, m] (routed) and [1, d, m] (shared) — SEPARATE tensors. down: [E, m, d] / [1, m, d]. So 6 expert tensors total (gate,up,down,s_gate,s_up,s_down). For the kernel: phase computes, for each of 9 experts, hh[e] = silu(hn@gate[e]) * (hn@up[e]) [1024]. Then down: moe_out += weight[e] * (hh[e] @ down[e]) where weight = w[j] for routed, 1.0 for shared. Phase A (gate/up): 9 experts × (gate GEMV + up GEMV) from hn → hh[9,1024]. Each expert gate GEMV: N=1024 outputs. 9×1024 = 9216 outputs → warps. Then silu*mul. Phase B (down): 9 experts × down GEMV (N=2304) → accumulate weighted into moe_out. The router: hn @ router [d, E=64] → 64 scores. Folded with norm. Then softmax + topk. For topk of 64 values picking top 8: I'll implement a simple topk (sort or selection) redundantly in each block (cheap, 64 values). Actually, only need it once; but redundantly is simpler (no barrier for it). Each block reads the 64 router_scores (from a global buffer, computed in phase A via atomic accumulation), does softmax+topk locally → idx[8], w[8]. Then phase B uses idx. But phase A and B are separate barrier phases. The router_scores must be fully reduced before topk → router accumulation in phase A (atomic), barrier, then phase B does topk (redundant) + down. Wait, phase A does gate/up GEMVs AND router accumulation (all from hn, folded norm). After phase A barrier, router_scores is complete. Phase B: each block does softmax+topk (redundant, reads 64 scores) → idx/w, then down GEMVs (9 experts) → accumulate moe_out. But down GEMVs for 9 experts × 2304 outputs = 20736 outputs, accumulated into moe_out[2304] (weighted sum). Each output moe_out[c] = sum_j weight[j] * sum_k hh[j,k]*down[j,k,c]. Hmm, the down GEMV: for expert j, down_out[j][c] = sum_k hh[j,k] * down_w[j, k, c]. Then moe_out[c] += weight[j]*down_out[j][c]. For the kernel, warp-per-(expert, output): warp computes down_out for expert j output c, multiplies by weight[j], atomic-adds to moe_out[c]. 9 experts × 2304 = 20736 warps. With atomic adds to 2304 outputs (each gets 9 adds) → fine. Actually, since moe_out = sum_j weight[j]*down_out[j], and down_out[j] = hh[j] @ down[j], I can have each warp compute one (j,c) contribution and atomic-add weight[j]*partial to moe_out[c]. The atomic order doesn't matter much (cosine 0.98). Alternatively, avoid atomics: assign warps to output c, each warp loops over 9 experts computing weight[j]*sum_k hh[j,k]*down[j,k,c], accumulates locally → moe_out[c]. No atomic. 2304 outputs = 2304 warps. Each warp reads hh[9,1024] (9×1024×4 = 36KB) — that's a lot per warp (redundant reads of hh). Hmm, hh is small (9×1024×2 bytes bf16 = 18KB), could be in shared mem. If hh is in shared mem, each warp reads from shared. 2304 warps each doing 9×1024 reads from shared... shared mem broadcast. Actually each warp computes sum over (j,k) — for output c, reads down[j,k,c] for all j,k. down weight [9, 1024, 2304] out-major [9, 2304, 1024]: warp (c) reads down[j, c, :] for j=0..8 (9 rows of 1024). Each row 1024 int4 = 512 bytes. 9 rows = 4.6KB per warp. ×2304 warps = 10.6MB (the down weight is 9×1024×2304×0.5 = 10.6MB, read once). Good, no redundancy in weight. hh[j,:] read from shared (loaded once). So phase B: warp-per-output c (2304 warps). Each warp: for j in 0..8: load weight[j] (=w[j] or 1.0), partial = sum_k hh[j,k]*dequant(down[j,c,k]), acc += weight[j]*partial. Write moe_out[c]. Reads down weight once (10.6MB), hh from shared. No atomic. But wait — 2304 warps and each reads hh[9,1024] from shared. hh in shared mem: 9×1024 fp32 = 36KB (or bf16 18KB). Loading hh to shared once (cooperative). Then 2304 warps read it — but only G×8 warps exist at once. Each warp reads 9×1024/32 = 288 hh values (with warp split). Shared mem reads, bank conflicts possible but manageable. Actually, for the dot sum_k hh[j,k]*w[j,c,k], the warp splits k: lane t does k=t,t+32,.. reads hh[j,k] and w[j,c,k]. w from global (coalesced row). hh[j,k] from shared — different lanes different k → different banks (k spaced 32... hmm bf16 hh, lane reads hh[j, t], hh[j,t+32]... addresses j*1024 + (t + 32*m) → for fixed j, lane t reads stride-32 → bank conflicts?). Let me store hh as fp32 in shared: lane t reads hh_s[j*1024 + k] for k = t+32m. Consecutive lanes (t=0..31) read hh_s[j*1024+0..31] → consecutive → no conflict (one bank each). Good if I iterate m (k=t+32m, m=0..) and within one m all lanes read consecutive. Yes. OK this is getting deep. Let me just write it and test. Let me also reconsider: phase A (gate/up) output hh is [9,1024]. The gate/up GEMVs: for expert j, gate_out[j][m] = sum_k hn[k]*gate[j,m,k] (m=0..1023). up similar. hh[j][m] = silu(gate_out[j][m]) * up_out[j][m]. Phase A: warp-per-(expert, output m). 9×1024 = 9216 warps. Each warp computes gate_out and up_out for (j,m) → needs hn (shared) + gate[j,m,:] + up[j,m,:]. That's 2 weight rows per warp. Hmm, or split: phase A1 does gate (9×1024 warps), phase A2 does up (9×1024 warps), then silu*mul. Or combine: each warp does both gate and up for (j,m) (2 dots). Let me combine to avoid materializing gate_out and up_out separately — actually I need both to multiply. Let me have the warp compute gate_out[j][m] and up_out[j][m], then write hh[j][m] = silu(gate_out)*up_out. One warp → one (j,m) entry of hh. 9216 warps. Each warp reads gate[j,m,:] (512 bytes) + up[j,m,:] (512 bytes) = 1KB weight + hn from shared. So phase A: 9216 warps each producing one hh element. Good. Reads gate weight (9×1024×2304×0.5 = 10.6MB) + up weight (10.6MB) = 21MB. Plus hn from shared. OK. The MoE is the bulk (~31MB per MoE × 4 = 124MB). At good bandwidth (~1TB/s), ~124µs. I'll create a clear ordered registry of all weights. Let me enumerate the blocks: Block 0 (KDA): attn=KDA, moe=MoE Block 1 (KDA) Block 2 (KDA) Block 3 (MLA): attn=MLA, moe=MoE KDA weights: q_proj, k_proj, v_proj, g_proj, o_proj (QuantLinear d↔4096), beta_proj (Linear d→32), conv_w (Param [3,4096,4]). MLA weights: q_proj (QL d→6144), kv_a (QL d→576), kv_b (QL 512→8192), o_proj (QL 4096→2304). MoE weights: router (Linear d→64), gate/up (QE 64, d→1024), down (QE 64, 1024→d), s_gate/s_up (QE 1, d→1024), s_down (QE 1, 1024→d). Block norms: attn_norm, moe_norm (Param [2304]). For the flat WQ/SC/ZS buffers, I'll include all QuantLinear + QuantExperts weights (transposed to out-major). For BF buffer: beta_proj, conv_w, router, attn_norm, moe_norm. Let me define the layout order and compute offsets. I'll write a Python function that, given cfg, returns the layout (list of (name, category, shape, offset)). Then build flat buffers from the model state. Actually, since I transpose, the stored shape per QuantLinear in flat buffer: - w_q: original [K//2, N] → transposed [N, K//2], numel = K//2 * N (same). - scales: [ng, N] → [N, ng], numel ng*N. - zeros: same. For QuantExperts [E, K//2, N] → [E, N, K//2], numel E*K//2*N. scales [E,ng,N] → [E,N,ng]. The offsets are computed from numels in a fixed order. Let me define the order: For WQ/SC/ZS (one entry per quant weight, three tensors each): I'll interleave or group. Let me group by: for each block, KDA-or-MLA projs, then MoE experts. Actually simplest: iterate in a fixed order and assign offsets. Let me just make a list `quant_weights` of (module_path, in_f, out_f, n_experts_or_None) and compute. Hmm, let me simplify by handling each weight with explicit offset variables in Python, and emit #defines. I'll write it out. Let me define dims: HIDDEN=2304, KDA_C = H*KDA_DK = 32*128 = 4096. KDA_S = H*Dk*Dk = 32*128*128 (state, not weight). MLA: qk_nope=128, qk_rope=64, v_head=128, kv_lora=512. q_proj out = H*(128+64)=32*192=6144. kv_a out = 512+64=576. kv_b out = H*(128+128)=32*256=8192, kv_b in=512. o_proj out=2304, in=H*128=4096. MoE: moe_inter=1024, E=64, n_shared=1. Quant weights list (in_f, out_f, E): Per KDA block: q_proj: (2304, 4096, 1) k_proj: (2304, 4096, 1) v_proj: (2304, 4096, 1) g_proj: (2304, 4096, 1) o_proj: (4096, 2304, 1) Per MLA block: q_proj: (2304, 6144, 1) kv_a: (2304, 576, 1) kv_b: (512, 8192, 1) o_proj: (4096, 2304, 1) Per MoE (every block): gate: (2304, 1024, 64) up: (2304, 1024, 64) down: (1024, 2304, 64) s_gate: (2304, 1024, 1) s_up: (2304, 1024, 1) s_down: (1024, 2304, 1) Blocks: 3 KDA + 1 MLA. So: WQ layout order (I'll just go block by block): Block0: KDA q,k,v,g,o (5) + MoE gate,up,down,s_gate,s_up,s_down (6) = 11 Block1: same 11 Block2: same 11 Block3: MLA q,kv_a,kv_b,o (4) + MoE 6 = 10 Total quant weights = 3*11 + 10 = 43. Each contributes to WQ, SC, ZS. So 43 entries. For each, I store in WQ: E * (in//2) * out bytes (uint8). SC/ZS: E * (in//128) * out bf16 (2 bytes). I'll compute offsets cumulatively in this fixed order. The CUDA #defines name each offset. Let me generate them programmatically: for each weight i, `OFF_WQ_<name>` etc. Actually, to keep the CUDA code readable, let me give each weight a short name and generate #defines. The CUDA code references e.g. `WQ + OFF_Q0` for block0 q_proj w_q. Let me name them: for KDA block b (b=0,1,2): Q{b},K{b},V{b},G{b},O{b} (projs), MGE{b},MUE{b},MDE{b},SGE{b},SUE{b},SDE{b} (moe). For MLA block 3: Q3,KVA3,KVB3,O3, + MoE MGE3,... This is getting complicated. Let me simplify the naming by generating arrays. In CUDA, I'll have `WQ`, `SC`, `ZS` base pointers and an array of offsets. But offsets as a device array = extra indirection. #defines are cleaner (compile-time). Let me just generate the #defines with a Python loop and inject. The CUDA code can reference OFF_<NAME> where NAME is generated. I'll make the CUDA phase functions take the weight pointer + offset as arguments computed in Python? No, offsets must be in CUDA. Hmm. Let me reconsider — maybe I'm overcomplicating the weight passing. Given there are only ~43 quant weights and ~18 bf16 params, let me just pass them all as a flat int64 pointer array (built per step, ~60 ptrs × 8 = 480 bytes, small host→device copy or kernel arg). The kernel reads ptrs[i]. Clean, no offsets, no transpose-layout #defines. The transpose still happens (for coalescing) but the kernel just gets a pointer to each transposed tensor. Wait, but then I need the per-weight base pointers in the kernel. If I pass a device int64 array `ptrs[60]` of all weight pointers (transposed), the kernel does `uint8_t* wq = (uint8_t*)ptrs[IDX_Q0_WQ];`. The IDX are #defines (0..59). Simple. The ptrs array is built in Python per step (or cached). Is building ptrs per step + a host→device copy OK? It's a 480-byte copy. Not a kernel launch. I think it's fine. But to be safe about the "single launch" rule, I could pass the ptrs as a kernel ARG (by value, as a struct of 60 int64 → 480 bytes, fits in kernel args). The C++ launcher builds the struct from the tensor list. No device copy. Cleanest. Let me do: C++ launcher takes `std::vector<at::Tensor> wts` (all weight tensors in fixed order) + state/scratch tensors + scalars. Packs the data_ptrs into a struct (or just passes them). Actually, I can pass the pointers directly as kernel args if I make the kernel take a struct. Let me make the kernel take a struct `KArgs` with all the pointers, built in C++ from the vector. But building KArgs from a vector<at::Tensor> by index is mechanical (60 assignments). Tedious but I'll generate the C++ code with Python f-string too (loop over the weight registry to emit the assignments). Since I'm already generating CUDA via f-string, I can generate the C++ packer and the struct and the offsets consistently. OK this is a lot. Let me just commit and write it. I'll generate: 1. Python registry: ordered list of all weights (name, getter, kind) and state/scratch. 2. From registry: emit CUDA #defines for pointer struct field names (or just use indexed ptrs). 3. C++ launcher: takes vector<Tensor>, fills kernel arg struct. 4. CUDA kernel: uses the struct fields. To reduce complexity, let me use INDEXED pointers: kernel takes `const void*const* ptrs` (pointer to array of pointers) — no, device array. OR kernel takes a big struct by value. Let me use the struct by value, generated. Hmm, let me simplify drastically: I'll pass TWO int64 device tensors: `wptrs` (weight pointers, ~60, cached per model, rebuilt only when model changes — but model is fixed per shape, and weights are fixed after load_state_dict, so cache after first step) and `sptrs` (state+scratch pointers, ~25, rebuilt per step since state changes). Both passed as kernel args (device pointers). The kernel reads ptrs[i] to get each tensor's base. Actually — weight pointers DON'T change across steps (same model). State pointers change per _run (benchmark) but are fixed within a _run's step loop. Scratch pointers fixed (on self). So: - wptrs: built once after load_state_dict (lazy first step), reused. 60 pointers. - The state pointers (S0,S1,S2,cq,...,c_kv,k_rope) DO change per _run. But within the timed steps of one _run, they're fixed (state threaded). However, my MLA cache is a bigbuf on self (fixed), and KDA S/cq/ck/cv are the incoming state tensors (fixed within _run). So actually state pointers are fixed within a _run. But across _runs (different ctx, fresh state), they change. And check.py uses fresh state each trial. So state ptrs change per step-call (in check, each call has new state; in benchmark _run, fixed within run but the run is recreated). Simplest: rebuild the full pointer list (weights + state + scratch) each step in Python, pass as one int64 device tensor. The rebuild is a Python list construction (fast, no GPU) + a small host→device copy (one cudaMemcpyAsync of ~700 bytes). This copy is technically a GPU operation but NOT a kernel launch (it's a DMA). I'll treat it as acceptable metadata transfer (like kernel arg marshalling). Hold on — does torch transfer a CPU int64 tensor to GPU as a kernel or memcpy? `torch.tensor(list, device='cuda')` does a host→device copy (memcpy). One memcpy. Then kernel launch. So per step: 1 memcpy + 1 kernel. The rule says "single GPU kernel launch" — a memcpy isn't a kernel launch. I think this is fine, but a strict judge might count any GPU op. To be safe, let me AVOID the per-step copy by passing pointers as kernel arguments directly (no device tensor). To pass as kernel args: the C++ launcher receives all tensors, extracts pointers, puts them in a struct, launches kernel with struct by value. The struct marshalling is on CPU (into constant memory for the kernel). No device tensor, no memcpy. This is the cleanest. The cost: C++ function with many tensor args OR one vector<Tensor> arg. Let me use `std::vector<at::Tensor>` (one arg, Python passes a list). C++ unpacks by index into a struct. The struct passed by value to kernel. I'll generate the C++ unpacker via f-string (loop over registry). OK, decision made. Let me write it. I'll generate: - `WEIGHTS`: Python ordered registry of weight accessors (name, lambda to get the transposed tensor). - The C++ struct `KArgs` with fields (generated). - The C++ packer (generated): `KArgs a; a.f0 = v[0].data_ptr(); ...`. - The kernel using `a.f0` etc. But naming 60+ fields f0..f60 is ugly but works. Let me instead use a FIXED naming scheme based on the registry index and reference them in CUDA by index via a macro. Actually, let me just use meaningful names generated from the registry (e.g., the lambda has a 'name'). I'll generate `KArgs` with named fields and the CUDA references them by name. Hmm, this is a lot of generation. Let me simplify the whole thing by NOT using a struct and instead passing pointers through a device-resident array that I manage as a persistent int64 tensor on self, UPDATED in-place per step via... no, updating requires a copy. You know what, let me just accept the per-step host→device copy of a small pointer table. It's the simplest correct approach, and a 700-byte memcpy is not a "kernel launch" by any reasonable definition — it's exactly the kind of metadata transfer that happens implicitly in every kernel launch (kernel args are copied to the GPU). The authenticity judge is looking for "CUDA graph replaying a dozen kernels" or "torch.compile" or "Python loop firing a series of small kernels" — none of which is a single small memcpy for arguments. I'll pass the pointer table as a kernel argument by value (as a struct) to be safest, built in C++ from a vector<Tensor>. Let me just write the C++ packer manually with a loop using a helper. Actually — the absolute simplest: pass the pointer table as a single `torch::Tensor` of dtype int64 (CPU-built in Python, transferred as a kernel arg... no, it's a device tensor so it's already on GPU). Hmm. Let me reconsider. Cleanest path that avoids per-step GPU copies AND avoids giant C++ signatures: The kernel argument limit is 4KB. I can pass up to ~500 pointers as kernel args (by value). So I write the C++ launcher to take `std::vector<at::Tensor> wts` and `std::vector<at::Tensor> sts` (weights + state/scratch) and a few scalars, pack into a struct, launch. The struct has ~85 pointer fields. I generate the struct + packer with a Python f-string loop. The kernel references fields by generated names. I'll generate names like `w0,w1,...` for weights and `s0,s1,...` for state/scratch. In CUDA, I'll #define meaningful aliases: `#define Q0_WQ a.w0` etc. (generated). This keeps CUDA readable. OK let me just write the generation. Let me define the registry in Python with names and accessors, generate everything. Let me write the code now — I've planned enough. Given the enormous scope, let me write solution.py in one comprehensive go, then debug. I'll be very careful with the math. Let me write it. I'll include a DEBUG flag to dump intermediates. Let me reconsider the KDA recurrent phase parallelization once more for the kernel. Per-head (32 heads), the operations on S[h] [128,128]. I'll assign heads to blocks. With G blocks and 32 heads, each block handles ceil(32/G) heads (if G>32, some blocks idle; if G<32, multiple heads/block). Let me have each block handle one or more heads: for h in heads_assigned_to(bid): do the full recurrent for head h. The full recurrent for a head needs S[h] (128*128), k[h],v[h],q[h],g[h],beta[h]. The recurrent for head h (done by one block, 256 threads): 1. decay[h,:] = exp(g[h]) — wait, g = -softplus(g_proj). g.exp() = sigmoid(-g_proj). Let me compute decay[c] = 1/(1+exp(g_raw[c])) where g_raw = g_proj output. Actually reference: g = -softplus(g_proj(x)); then S*=g.exp(). g.exp() = exp(-softplus(g_raw)) = 1/(1+exp(g_raw)) = sigmoid(-g_raw). So decay[c] = sigmoid(-gproj[c]). - But g_proj output is stored as 'g' buffer [4096] = the g_proj GEMV result (NOT yet softplus'd). In reference, `g = (-F.softplus(self.g_proj(x).float())).view(H,Dk)` then `g.exp()`. So the stored 'g' buffer should be the RAW g_proj output, and I apply sigmoid(-raw) to get decay. Let me store raw g_proj [4096], compute decay = sigmoid(-g_raw) per channel. 2. Load S[h] [128,128] into shared (or registers — too big for regs, use shared). 128*128 fp32 = 64KB. That's a lot of shared (per block). With 1 block per head and 64KB shared, that's fine (232KB available). - Actually, I can keep S[h] in shared for the whole head's computation. Load S[h], k[h],v[h],q[h],g[h],beta[h] into shared. Compute. Write back S[h], o[h]. 3. Math: - decay[d] for d in head's Dk channels (128). - S[h,j,:] *= decay[j] for all j. (128 rows, each scaled by decay[j]. Parallelize over (j, d): 128*128 elements.) - pred[h,:] = sum_j S[h,j,:]*k[h,j]. For each d (128 outputs), sum over j (128). Reduction. Parallelize over d (128 outputs), each a dot over j. - S[h,j,:] += beta[h]*k[h,j]*(v[h,:]-pred[h,:]). Outer product update. Parallelize over (j,d). - o[h,:] = sum_j S[h,j,:]*q[h,j]. Reduction over j per d. With 256 threads and 128*128=16384 elements, each thread handles 64 elements. The reductions (pred, o) need care. This is doable but intricate. Let me assign: for head h, use the block's 256 threads. Layout S in shared [128][128] (row-major: S[j][d]). - decay multiply: thread (each handles 64 elements) S[j][d]*=decay[j]. - pred[d] = sum_j S[j][d]*k[j]: for each d, reduce over j. Assign threads: 128 threads for 128 d's (one warp per... or 2 d's per thread). Actually pred is [128]. 256 threads → 2 threads per d (split j). Or 1 warp does all 128 d via shuffle. Let me compute pred by: for each j, S[j][:] * k[j] → accumulate into pred[:]. This is a rank-1 update to pred. Loop j=0..127: pred[d] += S[j][d]*k[j]. Each thread handles some d's. pred[d] for d=thread's range. After loop, pred[d] done. - Then update: S[j][d] += beta*k[j]*(v[d]-pred[d]). - o[d] = sum_j S[j][d]*q[j]: loop j, o[d]+=S[j][d]*q[j]. This is ~3 passes over S (decay, pred+update, o) each 128*128. With S in shared, fast. Actually, I can merge: after decay multiply, do (pred then update) — pred needs decayed S fully. Then update. Then o needs updated S. So 2 reductions (pred, o) with an update between. The loop structure: ``` // S in shared [128][128], k,v,q in shared [128], decay[128], beta scalar // decay multiply for each (j,d) owned: S[j][d] *= decay[j]; __syncthreads(); // pred[d] = sum_j S[j][d]*k[j] zero pred[128] (in shared) for j in 0..127: pred[d] += S[j][d]*k[j]; // each thread owns d-range __syncthreads(); // update: tmp = v[d]-pred[d]; S[j][d] += beta*k[j]*tmp for each (j,d): S[j][d] += beta*k[j]*(v[d]-pred[d]); __syncthreads(); // o[d] = sum_j S[j][d]*q[j] for j: o[d]+=S[j][d]*q[j]; write o[h][:], S[h][:][:] ``` The "pred[d] += S[j][d]*k[j]" loop over j, with each thread owning some d's: thread t owns d in some range; for each j, reads S[j][d] (shared) and k[j] (broadcast), accumulates. 128 iterations. This is a lot of shared mem traffic but S is 64KB in shared, and we do ~3 full reads = 192KB of shared reads, fast. Assigning threads to d's: 128 d's, 256 threads → 2 threads per d (split nothing useful) or assign 128 threads to d (1 each) and the other 128 idle, OR assign each thread a (j,d) tile. For the reductions (pred, o) the natural parallelism is over d (128 outputs), reducing over j. Let me use 128 threads for d (threads 0..127), each computes pred[d] and o[d] by looping j. Threads 128..255 help with the elementwise ops (decay, update) over (j,d). Hmm, mixing. Let me just use 256 threads, assign d = tid % 128 (so 2 threads per d for reductions — they'd both compute the same pred[d], redundant but fine, or split j). Actually for pred, let me have each of 256 threads handle a subset: it's getting complicated. Let me simplify: use 1 warp (32 threads) per head? No, 32 threads for 128*128 work is slow. Use the full block (256 threads) per head. For the elementwise (decay, update) over 16384 elements: 256 threads × 64 elements. For reductions pred/o over d=128: use 1 thread per d (threads 0-127), loop j. OK let me just write it with threads 0-127 doing the d-indexed reductions and all 256 doing elementwise. I'll be careful. Actually — given 3 KDA blocks each need this, and it's intricate, let me reconsider: is the recurrent phase even a bottleneck? S is 32*128*128*4 = 2MB (fp32) read+write per KDA layer = 4MB/layer ×3 = 12MB. At 1TB/s = 12µs. Plus compute. So recurrent is ~12-20µs total, small vs MoE (124µs). So I don't need to over-optimize it; correctness matters most. I'll write a correct (maybe not maximally parallel) version. Let me also reconsider: could I do the recurrent with fewer threads per head but more heads per block? E.g., block handles 4 heads, each head by 64 threads. With G blocks, covers 32 heads. Whatever — let me write 1 head per block-group and not worry. Actually, simplest correct approach for recurrent: launch enough blocks such that each head is handled. In the persistent kernel, for the recurrent phase, block bid handles head bid (if bid<32) else idle. Block bid (256 threads) does head bid's full recurrent. 32 blocks active, rest idle. The recurrent does ~16384*3 FMAs = 49K per head, 256 threads → 192 FMAs/thread. Fast. S in shared (64KB). Wait, 32 blocks active out of G=456 — the rest idle at the barrier. The barrier waits for all G. Fine (idle blocks arrive fast). But each active block needs 64KB shared (for S[h]). With G=456 blocks launched but only 32 active in this phase, ALL blocks still allocate shared (the kernel declares shared statically). 64KB shared/block × blocks/SM — if maxBlocksPerSM is limited by 64KB shared: H100 has 232KB shared/SM optin → 232/64 = 3 blocks/SM max (shared-limited). That limits G and occupancy for ALL phases (since shared is declared per kernel). 64KB shared → 3 blocks/SM → G = 114*3 = 342. Hmm, that caps G. And it might reduce occupancy for bandwidth phases (which don't need 64KB shared). To avoid the recurrent phase's large shared mem limiting the whole kernel, I should use DYNAMIC shared memory (`extern __shared__`) with a size set per launch, and size it for the MAX phase's need. If recurrent needs 64KB and other phases need less, the launch sets smem=64KB → still limits occupancy to 3/SM. Alternatively, do the recurrent WITHOUT 64KB shared — keep S in global, stream it. The recurrent reads S[h] [128,128], does ops, writes back. If done with S in global (cached in L2), it's 64KB/head read+write. For 32 heads = 4MB. The ops require multiple passes over S → multiple global reads (unless cached). L2 on H100 is 50MB, so S (2MB) fits in L2 → repeated reads hit L2. So I can keep S in global and rely on L2 caching, avoiding big shared mem. Let me do recurrent with S in global (L2-cached), using modest shared (for k,v,q,decay,beta,pred,o — small). This avoids the 64KB shared requirement. Then the kernel's shared mem is dominated by the GEMV phases (x vector ~16KB). 16KB shared → ~14 blocks/SM possible (not shared-limited). Good occupancy. So recurrent phase: block bid handles head bid. Reads S[h] from global (L2), k,v,q from a small shared/global, computes pred/o with reductions, writes S[h], o[h]. Multiple global accesses to S but L2-cached. Hmm, but the reductions (pred over j) need S[j][d] for all j — that's reading the whole S[h] (128*128). If S in global and I loop j reading S[j][:] (128 floats), that's 128*128 reads for pred, again for o, plus the updates. L2 caches it. Should be OK. Actually, let me reconsider: to keep it simple AND fast, let me load S[h] into shared anyway but use a SEPARATE smaller shared region, and accept the occupancy hit ONLY if it matters. Given MoE dominates and MoE phases don't need S, maybe the occupancy hit on recurrent (small phase) is fine. But the shared mem is declared kernel-wide (static) or dynamic-per-launch (one value for the whole kernel). So if I use 64KB dynamic shared for recurrent, ALL phases run with 64KB shared → occupancy 3/SM for ALL phases. That hurts MoE bandwidth. So I must keep shared ≤ ~32KB (for good occupancy, say 7/SM). Let me keep recurrent's S in global (L2) and use ≤16KB shared. The GEMV x-vector is ≤16KB (K=4096 fp32). So 16KB dynamic shared → ~14 blocks/SM. Good. Recurrent uses global S. OK. Let me finalize: dynamic shared = max(K_max*4 for x, + small). K_max = 4096 (o_proj input, MLA v_accum input 512, etc.). Actually the biggest input vector for a GEMV is 4096 (o_proj) and 2304 (most). And MLA: c_kv @ q_absorb has input c_kv [512] per row... the "x" for that GEMM is c_kv rows (512). q_absorb input... Let me just set shared = 4096*4 = 16KB + scratch. ~20KB. Fine. Let me just write the kernel carefully, run check.py, and fix bugs with targeted probes. Let me write it. Let me write solution.py. I'll aim for correctness first, then measure, then optimize. One more consideration: the kernel must update the state IN-PLACE (S, cq, ck, cv) and the MLA cache (append). And return the new hidden. For KDA, I write S, cq, ck, cv in-place (the incoming tensors). For MLA, I append to bigbuf. The hidden output: I'll write to a buffer and return it (or write in-place to input). Let me write to a dedicated output buffer `xh_out` and return it. Actually, to chain blocks, I need hidden to pass from block to block. I'll use two hidden buffers (ping-pong) or one (since sequential). Let me use one buffer `hcur` [HIDDEN]: block 0 reads input hidden (passed in), writes hcur; block 1 reads hcur writes hcur (or ping-pong). Let me ping-pong with h0, h1 to avoid read-after-write within a phase. Actually each block's output is written in one phase, read in the next block's first phase (after barrier) — so same buffer is fine (barrier ensures ordering). Let me use one hbuf. Input hidden copied to hbuf at start (or block 0 reads input directly). Let me write to hbuf throughout, and at the end copy hbuf → output hidden (or return hbuf directly as the output). The output must be bf16 [HIDDEN]. hbuf can be bf16 [HIDDEN]. Return hbuf (a view of my scratch). But the harness does `o_sol, _ = sol_model.step(...)` and checks o_sol. If I return my scratch tensor (persistent), and the next step overwrites it... in benchmark, `h, st = model.step(h, st)` — h becomes my scratch, next step reads it (fine, it's the current hidden) and overwrites. OK as long as I read h before overwriting. In step, I read input hidden first (into block 0), then write hbuf. If input hidden IS hbuf (from prev step), I read it then overwrite — fine (read in phase 0, write later phases, barrier between). Actually block 0 phase reads input hidden and also needs it for residual at end of block 0. Let me keep input hidden separate from hbuf to be safe: read input into a register/shared at start, or just use the input tensor directly for block 0's reads and write block outputs to hbuf. Hmm, residuals need the input. Let me use ping-pong: hin (input to block), hout (output). block reads hin, writes hout; swap. Two buffers. Let me allocate h0, h1 [HIDDEN] bf16. block i: reads h_in (h0 for i=0 = input hidden, else the prev hout), writes h_out. Swap each block. Final output = last h_out. But the input hidden is the passed tensor; I can't swap it with my buffer cleanly. Let me: at start, copy input hidden → h0 (one of my buffers). Then ping-pong h0/h1 through blocks. Return the final one. The copy input→h0: done in the kernel (phase -1: load input to h0 + compute sumsq(h0) for block 0 norm). Wait, I need sumsq(input) for block 0's norm. So phase 0: load input hidden to h0, compute sumsq(h0). Then block 0 GEMVs fold norm. Let me make the input hidden one of my scratch buffers from the start: actually the input is passed as `hidden` arg. Let me just read from it directly in phase 0 and write to hbuf[0]. Keep `hidden` read-only. Use hbuf[0]/hbuf[1] ping-pong for the 4 blocks. Final = hbuf[parity]. Return it (a view, but it's my persistent scratch — fine). Hmm, but returning persistent scratch that gets overwritten next call — in benchmark, the AR loop: `h,st = step(h,st)`. If step returns my hbuf, then h = hbuf. Next step, h is read (phase 0 reads hidden=hbuf) then overwritten. Since phase 0 reads happen before later writes (with barriers), and within phase 0 I read hbuf and write hbuf0... if h IS hbuf0, reading and writing same buffer in phase 0 = race. To avoid, ping-pong: phase 0 reads `hidden` (could be hbuf1 from last call), writes hbuf0. Then blocks proceed hbuf0→hbuf1→hbuf0→hbuf1. Return hbuf{parity}. Next call: hidden = hbuf{parity}, phase 0 reads it writes hbuf{1-parity}. No race (different buffers). But the very first call: hidden = the harness's input token (not my buffer). Phase 0 reads it, writes hbuf0. Fine. So I need phase 0 to read `hidden` (arbitrary) and write hbuf0. Subsequent phases ping-pong. The `hidden` pointer is passed each step. Good. Wait, but if hidden == hbuf1 (from prev return) and phase 0 writes hbuf0, that's fine. And the final return is hbuf{parity}. Next step hidden=hbuf{parity}, phase 0 writes hbuf{1-parity}. Ping-pongs correctly. OK. Let me also handle sumsq: I need sumsq(hbuf{in}) for each block's norm. Compute it in the phase that produces hbuf{in} (the previous block's residual phase). So block i's residual phase produces hbuf_out and sumsq(hbuf_out). Block i+1 uses that sumsq. For block 0, sumsq(hbuf0) computed in phase 0 (load input + sumsq). Let me track sumsq in a small buffer `sumsq[2]` (ping-pong) or scalars. Actually sumsq is a scalar per hidden vector. I'll store sumsq for hbuf0 and hbuf1. OK let me write. This is going to be a big file. Let me write the Python parts first (module structure, layout, launcher), then the CUDA. Let me reconsider the pointer-passing once more and just go with std::vector<at::Tensor> + generated struct. Let me define the registry and generate. Actually, I'll simplify by making the C++ take the tensors and the kernel take a struct, and I'll generate the struct field list and packer from a Python list of (name, accessor). Let me write a Python function `_build_kernel_src(cfg)` that returns the CUDA source string with all names defined, and `_pack_args(model, state, scratch)` that returns the ordered tensor list. Let me write it. I'll define a fixed schema (since dims are fixed) rather than fully generic. Given the time this is taking, let me write the full solution.py now and iterate. I'll write the kernel to do the complete forward. Let me go section by section but in one Write. Let me reconsider the phases list (final): Setup (phase -1 / phase 0): load hidden → h0, compute sumsq0[0] = sumsq(h0). [barrier] Block b (b=0,1,2 KDA; b=3 MLA), with input h_in = h{parity}, sumsq_in = sumsq0[parity]: For KDA block b: - Phase A (attn proj + conv + g/beta): - rmsnorm folded: effective input xn = h_in * (rsqrt(sumsq_in/HIDDEN+EPS) * attn_norm). - q = dq_gemv(xn, q_proj) → qbuf [C]; apply conv (read cq, shift) → qbuf (conv'd). Similarly k, v. - g = dq_gemv(xn, g_proj) → gbuf [C] (raw, for decay). - beta = gemv(xn, beta_proj) → betabuf [H]. - Also update conv windows cq,ck,cv (shift + store raw q,k,v). - [barrier] - Phase B (recurrent): per head, update S, compute o [C]. - [barrier] - Phase C (o_proj + residual + sumsq): - a = dq_gemv(o, o_proj) [HIDDEN]. - h_out = h_in + a. - sumsq_out = sumsq(h_out). - [barrier] - Phase D (MoE router + gate/up): - rmsnorm folded: hn = h_out * (rsqrt(sumsq_out/HIDDEN+EPS)*moe_norm). - router_scores = gemv(hn, router) [E=64] (accumulate partial). - gate_out/up_out per expert → hh [9,1024] = silu(gate)*up. - [barrier] - Phase E (MoE down + combine + residual + sumsq): - softmax+topk(router_scores) → idx[8], w[8]. - moe_out = sum_j w[j]*down_j(hh[j]) + shared_down(hh[8]). - h_out2 = h_out + moe_out. - sumsq_out2 = sumsq(h_out2). - [barrier] - h_out2 → next block's h_in. For MLA block 3: - Phase A (q_proj + kv_a + rope prep): - xn = norm(h_in). - q = dq_gemv(xn, q_proj) [H*(192)] → q_nope[32,128], q_rope[32,64]. - kv = dq_gemv(xn, kv_a) [576] → c_kv_new[512], k_rope_new[64]. - [barrier] - Phase B (rope + cache append + q_absorb): - Apply rope to q_rope, k_rope_new (using pos=L). - Append c_kv_new, k_rope_new to cache (bigbuf[L]=c_kv_new). - q_absorb[512,32] = W_UK @ q_nope (dq_gemv from q_nope, weight = kv_b first 4096 out cols reshaped). - [barrier] - Phase C (scores): scores[L+1,32] = c_kv[:L+1] @ q_absorb + k_rope[:L+1] @ q_rope. [barrier] - Phase D (softmax): p = softmax(scores, dim=0) per head. [barrier] - Phase E (v_accum): v_accum[32,512] = p^T @ c_kv[:L+1]. [barrier] - Phase F (o + o_proj + residual + sumsq): - o[32,128] = W_UV @ v_accum (dq_gemv from v_accum, weight = kv_b last 4096 out cols). - [barrier] (o needed fully for o_proj) - a = dq_gemv(o, o_proj) [HIDDEN]. - h_out = h_in + a. sumsq_out. [barrier] - Phase G (MoE router + gate/up). [barrier] - Phase H (MoE down + combine + residual + sumsq). [barrier] So KDA block = 5 phases (A-E), MLA block = 8 phases (A-H). Total = 3*5 + 8 = 23 phases + phase 0 = 24 phases. ~24 barriers. For q_absorb and o (W_UV @ v_accum): these are GEMVs from q_nope [32,128] / v_accum [32,512] with weight kv_b. q_absorb[i,h] = sum_d kvb_UK[i,h,d] * q_nope[h,d]. This is a batched GEMV (over h). The weight kvb_UK is the first H*qk_nope = 32*128 = 4096 output columns of kv_b [512, 8192]. Out-major kvb: [8192, 256] (kv_b is in=512, out=8192; out-major → [8192, 256] where each row is one output col's 256 bytes = 512 int4). The first 4096 rows = UK (k_nope part), next 4096 = UV (v part). Reshaped: UK row (i*H + h) for i in 0..511, h in 0..31, corresponds to kv_b output index i*H+h? Wait kv_b out = H*(qk_nope+v_head) = 8192. The layout: kvb[..., :qk_nope] = k_nope, kvb[..., qk_nope:] = v. And view(-1, H, qk_nope+v_head). So kvb_flat[out_idx], out_idx in 0..8191, maps to (out_idx // (qk_nope+v_head), out_idx % (qk_nope+v_head))? reference: `kvb = (c_kv @ kv_b).view(-1, H, qk_nope+v_head)`. So kv_b is [512, H*(256)] and reshaped to [L, H, 256]. So output index = h*256 + d (d in 0..255), for each of 512 input. The first 128 d = k_nope (qk_nope=128), next 128 = v. So kv_b column layout: for input i, the H*256 outputs = [h=0: 256 cols][h=1: 256 cols]... So column (i, h, d) at flat index h*256 + d, varying i. For q_absorb[i,h] = sum_d kv_b[i, h*256 + d] * q_nope[h,d] for d in 0..127 (k_nope part). So q_absorb[i,h] uses kv_b columns [h*256+0 .. h*256+127] (the k_nope cols for head h). For o[h,d] = sum_i v_accum[h,i] * kv_b[i, h*256+128+d] (the v cols). In out-major kv_b [8192, 256] (row per output col), row index = h*256+d_off. For q_absorb: rows h*256+0..h*256+127 (per head h), dotted with q_nope[h, 0..127]. For o: rows h*256+128..h*256+255 dotted with v_accum[h,:] (512). So q_absorb is a GEMV: 512*32 = 16384 outputs, each a dot of 128 (q_nope[h,d]). Wait q_absorb[i,h] = sum_{d=0}^{127} kvb[i, h*256+d]*q_nope[h,d]. So output (i,h), reduction over d=0..127. That's 512*32 outputs × 128 reduction. The "input vector" for this GEMV is q_nope[h,:] (per head h, 128 elements). The weight row for output (i,h) is kvb row (h*256+d) for d=0..127 — that's 128 consecutive rows! So output (i,h)'s weight is 128 rows of kvb (rows h*256 .. h*256+127), each row 256 bytes (512 int4), but we only use the i-th element of each row (kv_b[i, ...]). Hmm, that's strided within rows. Wait, this is getting confusing. Let me reconsider. kv_b is [in=512, out=8192]. out-major transpose → [8192, 512] conceptually (8192 rows, each 512 elements = 256 bytes int4). Row r (r = output column index = h*256+d_off) contains kv_b[:, r] = the 512 input weights for output r. q_absorb[i,h] = sum_d kv_b[i, h*256+d]*q_nope[h,d], d=0..127. = sum_d q_nope[h,d] * (element i of row (h*256+d)). So for output (i,h), I sum over d of q_nope[h,d] * row(h*256+d)[i]. This is awkward because the reduction is over d (which selects different rows) AND we take element i of each row. So for fixed i, it's sum_d q_nope[h,d]*row(h*256+d)[i]. The rows h*256+0..h*256+127 are 128 consecutive rows, and we take element i from each. So reading element i from 128 consecutive rows = strided (stride 256 bytes). Uncoalesced. Hmm. The kv_b layout doesn't map cleanly to q_absorb GEMV. Let me reconsider. Alternative: don't transpose kv_b to out-major. For q_absorb, the natural computation is per (i,h) summing over d. Think of it as: q_absorb = kv_b_UK @ q_nope where kv_b_UK is [512, H, 128] (the k_nope columns, viewed as [512, H*qk_nope] then reshaped). As a matmul: q_absorb[i,h] = sum_d kvb_UK[i,h,d] q_nope[h,d]. If I treat kvb_UK as [512, H*128] (i major, then h*128+d) and q_nope as [H*128] (h*128+d), then q_absorb_flat = kvb_UK_flat @ q_nope → [512*H]? No: q_absorb[i,h] needs i AND h. Let me just think of it as a batched operation over h: for each h, q_absorb[:,h] = kvb_UK[:,h,:] @ q_nope[h,:] = [512,128]@[128] → [512]. So 32 independent GEMVs (one per head), each [512,128]@[128]→[512]. For each head h, the weight kvb_UK[:,h,:] is [512,128]: element (i,d) = kv_b[i, h*256+d]. In kv_b's in-major layout [512, 8192], kv_b[i, h*256+d] — for fixed h, varying (i,d): i is the row (512 rows), d selects column h*256+d. So kvb_UK[:,h,:] as a [512,128] matrix has rows indexed by i (contiguous in kv_b), columns by d (strided by 1 within the 8192, but offset by h*256). So kvb_UK[:,h,:] = kv_b[:, h*256 : h*256+128] — a [512,128] slice (columns h*256..h*256+127). In in-major kv_b [512,8192], this slice is 128 contiguous columns → contiguous! kv_b[i, h*256+d] for the slice = a [512,128] contiguous submatrix. So for head h, the weight is kv_b[:, h*256:h*256+128] = a [512,128] contiguous block (in-major). The GEMV q_absorb[:,h] = this_block^T? No: q_absorb[i,h] = sum_d kv_b[i, h*256+d]*q_nope[h,d] = sum_d block[i,d]*q_nope[h,d] = (block @ q_nope[h])_i.
