P_HH is [9, 1024] (hh), slots 0..7 routed (from idx), slot 8 shared. down weights: routed B{b}DOWN [64, HID, M/2] out-major (per expert e: [HID, M/2]). shared B{b}SDOWN [1, HID, M/2]. idx/w in P_IDXW [16] (idx[0..7], w[0..7]). down phase: warp c (output hidden index), loops slots. For c in [gwarp, ...]: acc=0 for j in 0..7: e = idxw[j]; wj=idxw[8+j] # dot hh[j][k] * down[e][c][k], k=0..1023 acc += wj * dot # shared acc += 1.0 * dot(hh[8], sdown[0][c]) HOUT[c] = HIN[c] + acc The dot over k=1023 with 32 lanes (warp). down[e][c][k] = down_wq[e*HID*(M/2) + c*(M/2) + k/2] (packed). out-major [e, HID, M/2]. For warp c: read down_wq row (e, c) = M/2=512 bytes. lanes split k. dequant with scales[e][c][k/128]... wait down scales [64, HID, ng=8] out-major → [e, HID, 8]. scales[e][c][g], g=k/128. Hmm, the down weight is [M(in=1024), HID(out)] per expert. Out-major transposed → [HID(out), M/2(in bytes)]. scales original [E, ng=8, HID] → transpose(1,2) → [E, HID, 8]. So scales[e][c][g]. For warp c, expert e: read row down_wq[e][c] (512 bytes = M int4), scales[e][c][0..7] (8 bf16), zeros. dot with hh[j][0..M-1]. hh[j] is [1024] (M). In global. L2-cached (9*1024*4=36KB). For warp c looping j (9 slots), reads hh[j][:] each. This is doable. Let me write a specialized down warp function. Returns reduced acc (valid on lane 0, but I'll warp_sum). Then down phase: warp c, for each slot, get wq_row = down_wq + e*HID*(M/2) + c*(M/2), sc_row = down_sc + e*HID*8 + c*8, etc. dot with hh[slot]. Let me write dq_dot_k as a warp-level dot (each lane does K/32, warp_sum). Then down phase loops slots. But dq_dot_k with x=hh[slot] (1024), and wq_row (512 bytes). 32 lanes, K/32=32 each. So lanes read bytes 0,0,1,1,2,2,...,15,15 then +16. Not coalesced nicely (pairs). Hmm. The out-major row is M/2=512 bytes contiguous. 32 lanes reading it: if lane t reads k=32*m+t, byte=(32*m+t)/2 = 16m + t/2. So bytes 0,0,1,1,... for t=0..31 (m=0): bytes 0..15 (each twice). Since byte holds 2 int4 (k=2b, 2b+1), lane t with k=2b (even) reads byte b low nibble, lane t+1 with k=2b+1 reads byte b high nibble. So 2 lanes share a byte. The byte load is shared. Addresses: byte t, t+32, t+64,... → stride 32 between lanes (t=0..31 → bytes 0..31) → coalesced (32 consecutive bytes = one transaction)! Yes. So lane t reads bytes t, t+32, ... (stride 32 across lanes → coalesced). Each byte → 2 k's. So dq_dot_k: lane t, for b=t, t+32, ... (b < M/2): byte=wq_row[b]; k0=2b; dequant w0 (k0), w1 (k0+1) with group g=k0/128 = b/64. scale= sc_row[b/64] (since group=128 k = 64 bytes). acc += x[k0]*w0 + x[k0+1]*w1. Wait group size 128 along K(in=M=1024). g = k/128. For byte b, k0=2b, g=2b/128=b/64. So every 64 bytes, new group. scales[g] for g=0..7 (8 groups for M=1024). sc_row[g] = scales for group g. So lane reads sc_row[b/64]. Good. So dq_dot_k(wq_row [M/2 bytes], sc_row [8], zr_row[8], x[M]): lane t loops b=t,t+32,..: ... acc += x[2b]*((lo-zr[b/64])*sc[b/64]) + x[2b+1]*((hi-zr[b/64])*sc[b/64]). warp_sum. This is clean and coalesced. Let me use this dot for the down phase. Actually, I can use the SAME dot pattern for the main gemv_om! Actually my gemv_om already does this (bi = g*64 + c*32 + lane, c=0,1 → bytes g*64+lane, g*64+32+lane = stride 32 across lanes within 64-byte group). --- a//home/shadeform/mega/outputs/runs/20260721_125907_zai-claude_glm-5.2_02_kimi_linear_decode/repo/problems/02_kimi_linear_decode/solution.py
+++ b//home/shadeform/mega/outputs/runs/20260721_125907_zai-claude_glm-5.2_02_kimi_linear_decode/repo/problems/02_kimi_linear_decode/solution.py
@@ -1,71 +1,168 @@
-"""solution.py placeholder: module structure only (for load_state_dict test)."""
+"""Fused single-kernel W4A16 decode for the Kimi-Linear hybrid unit (batch=1).
+
+The whole per-token forward (3 KDA + 1 MLA layer, each with a 64-expert MoE,
+all int4 dequant-GEMVs, short causal conv, KDA recurrent-state update, MLA
+latent-cache absorb attention, MoE router + expert GEMVs, both RMSNorms and
+residual adds) is one custom CUDA __global__ kernel built with load_inline and
+invoked exactly once in step(). No CUDA graph, no torch.compile, no per-op loop.
+
+int4 weights are streamed once through a fused dequant-GEMV (warp-per-output,
+out-major layout, per-group dequant in registers); the bf16 weight is never
+materialized. MLA uses the absorb form so kv_b is never materialized across the
+context. RMSNorm is folded into each GEMV's input load (sum-of-squares inline).
… +203 more lines

Let me write the Python generation: ```python def _kda_block(b, hin, hout, SP, CQP, CKP, CVP): return f""" // ===== KDA block {b} ===== // phase A: attn proj + conv + g/beta (norm folded) load_fold(sx, (bf16*)P[{hin}], BF+BF_AN{b}, HID, red); gemv_om_conv(sx, WQ+WQ_K{b}Q, SC+SC_K{b}Q, ZS+ZS_K{b}Q, HID, C, (float*)P[P_Q], bid, gridDim.x, (bf16*)P[{CQP}], BF+BF_CONV{b}+0*C*4); __syncthreads(); gemv_om_conv(sx, WQ+WQ_K{b}K, SC+SC_K{b}K, ZS+ZS_K{b}K, HID, C, (float*)P[P_K], bid, gridDim.x, (bf16*)P[{CKP}], BF+BF_CONV{b}+1*C*4); __syncthreads(); gemv_om_conv(sx, WQ+WQ_K{b}V, SC+SC_K{b}V, ZS+ZS_K{b}V, HID, C, (float*)P[P_V], bid, gridDim.x, (bf16*)P[{CVP}], BF+BF_CONV{b}+2*C*4); __syncthreads(); gemv_om(sx, WQ+WQ_K{b}G, SC+SC_K{b}G, ZS+ZS_K{b}G, HID, C, (float*)P[P_G], bid, gridDim.x); __syncthreads(); gemv_bf(sx, BF+BF_BETA{b}, HID, H, (float*)P[P_BETA], bid, gridDim.x); gbar(cnt, phase++, gen, gridDim.x); // phase B: recurrent kda_recur((float*)P[{SP}], (float*)P[P_Q],(float*)P[P_K],(float*)P[P_V],(float*)P[P_G],(float*)P[P_O],(float*)P[P_BETA], smem, red, bid, gridDim.x); gbar(cnt, phase++, gen, gridDim.x); // phase C: o_proj + residual -> hout (sumsq not needed; next block computes inline) load_fold(sx, (bf16*)P[P_O], (bf16*)0, C, red); gemv_om_res(sx, WQ+WQ_K{b}O, SC+SC_K{b}O, ZS+ZS_K{b}O, C, HID, (float*)P[{hout}], bid, gridDim.x, (bf16*)P[{hin}]); gbar(cnt, phase++, gen, gridDim.x); // phase D: moe router + shared gate/up load_fold(sx, (bf16*)P[{hout}], BF+BF_MN{b}, HID, red); gemv_bf(sx, BF+BF_ROUT{b}, HID, 64, (float*)P[P_ROUT], bid, gridDim.x); __syncthreads(); gemv_om_gateup(sx, WQ+WQ_B{b}SGATE, SC+SC_B{b}SGATE, ZS+ZS_B{b}SGATE, WQ+WQ_B{b}SUP, SC+SC_B{b}SUP, ZS+ZS_B{b}SUP, HID, M, (float*)P[P_HH]+8*M, bid, gridDim.x); gbar(cnt, phase++, gen, gridDim.x); // phase E: topk + routed gate/up moe_topk((float*)P[P_ROUT], (float*)P[P_IDXW], smem, bid); __syncthreads(); // routed experts 0..7 via idx moe_routed_gateup(sx, WQ+WQ_B{b}GATE, SC+SC_B{b}GATE, ZS+ZS_B{b}GATE, WQ+WQ_B{b}UP, SC+SC_B{b}UP, ZS+ZS_B{b}UP, HID, M, (float*)P[P_HH], (float*)P[P_IDXW], bid, gridDim.x); gbar(cnt, phase++, gen, gridDim.x); // phase F: down + combine + residual -> hout moe_down_resid(sx, WQ+WQ_B{b}DOWN, SC+SC_B{b}DOWN, ZS+ZS_B{b}DOWN, WQ+WQ_B{b}SDOWN, SC+SC_B{b}SDOWN, ZS+ZS_B{b}SDOWN, (float*)P[P_HH], (float*)P[P_IDXW], (bf16*)P[{hin}], (float*)P[{hout}], bid, gridDim.x); gbar(cnt, phase++, gen, gridDim.x); """ ``` Wait, the gemv_om_gateup for shared writes hh+8*M (slot 8). Let me write the helpers with fp32 scratch: ```cuda __device__ inline void load_fold(float* sx, const float* x, const bf16* wnorm, int K, volatile float* red){ int tid=threadIdx.x; if(wnorm){ float part=0; for(int i=tid;i<K;i+=THREADS){ float v=x[i]; sx[i]=v; part+=v*v; } __syncthreads(); float ss=block_sum(part,red); float rss=rsqrtf(ss/(float)HID+EPSF); for(int i=tid;i<K;i+=THREADS) sx[i]=sx[i]*b2f(wnorm[i])*rss; } else { for(int i=tid;i<K;i+=THREADS) sx[i]=x[i]; } __syncthreads(); } ``` gemv_om writes float y: ```cuda __device__ inline void gemv_om(const float* sx, const uint8_t* wq, const bf16* sc, const bf16* zr, int K, int N, float* y, int bid, int G){ ... y[out]=acc; // fp32 } ``` etc. All fp32 scratch. Let me set hp=h0. But to be safe, let me compute based on block count (4 → h0). I'll hardcode h0 (4 blocks even). Hmm, but if pattern had odd KDA it'd differ. Pattern is fixed KK KM = 4 blocks. block3 MLA writes h0. So output=h0. Let me write KDA template and MLA template. KDA(b, HIN, HOUT, SP, CQP, CKP, CVP): ``` // --- KDA block {b} --- // A: attn proj+conv+g+beta load_fold(sx,(float*)P[{HIN}],BF+BF_AN{b},HID,red); gemv_conv(sx,WQ+WQ_K{b}Q,SC+SC_K{b}Q,ZS+ZS_K{b}Q,HID,C,(float*)P[P_Q],bid,(bf16*)P[{CQP}],BF+BF_CONV{b}+0*C*4); __syncthreads(); gemv_conv(sx,WQ+WQ_K{b}K,...,(float*)P[P_K],bid,(bf16*)P[{CKP}],BF+BF_CONV{b}+1*C*4); __syncthreads(); gemv_conv(sx,WQ+WQ_K{b}V,...,(float*)P[P_V],bid,(bf16*)P[{CVP}],BF+BF_CONV{b}+2*C*4); __syncthreads(); gemv_om(sx,WQ+WQ_K{b}G,...,HID,C,(float*)P[P_G],bid); __syncthreads(); gemv_bf(sx,BF+BF_BETA{b},HID,H,(float*)P[P_BETA],bid); gbar(cnt,phase++,gen); // B: recur kda_recur((float*)P[{SP}],(float*)P[P_Q],(float*)P[P_K],(float*)P[P_V],(float*)P[P_G],(float*)P[P_O],(float*)P[P_BETA],smem,red,bid); gbar(cnt,phase++,gen); // C: o_proj + residual -> {HOUT} load_fold(sx,(float*)P[P_O],(bf16*)0,C,red); gemv_res(sx,WQ+WQ_K{b}O,...,C,HID,(float*)P[{HOUT}],bid,(float*)P[{HIN}]); gbar(cnt,phase++,gen); // D: moe router + shared gateup load_fold(sx,(float*)P[{HOUT}],BF+BF_MN{b},HID,red); gemv_bf(sx,BF+BF_ROUT{b},HID,64,(float*)P[P_ROUT],bid); __syncthreads(); gemv_gateup(sx,WQ+WQ_B{b}SGATE,SC+SC_B{b}SGATE,ZS+ZS_B{b}SGATE,WQ+WQ_B{b}SUP,SC+SC_B{b}SUP,ZS+ZS_B{b}SUP,HID,M,(float*)P[P_HH]+8*M,bid); gbar(cnt,phase++,gen); // E: topk + routed gateup moe_topk((float*)P[P_ROUT],(float*)P[P_IDXW],smem,bid); __syncthreads(); moe_routed_gateup(sx,WQ+WQ_B{b}GATE,SC+SC_B{b}GATE,ZS+ZS_B{b}GATE,WQ+WQ_B{b}UP,SC+SC_B{b}UP,ZS+ZS_B{b}UP,HID,M,(float*)P[P_HH],(float*)P[P_IDXW],bid); gbar(cnt,phase++,gen); // F: down + combine + residual -> {HOUT} moe_down_resid(sx,WQ+WQ_B{b}DOWN,SC+SC_B{b}DOWN,ZS+ZS_B{b}DOWN,WQ+WQ_B{b}SDOWN,SC+SC_B{b}SDOWN,ZS+ZS_B{b}SDOWN,(float*)P[P_HH],(float*)P[P_IDXW],(float*)P[{HIN}],(float*)P[{HOUT}],bid); gbar(cnt,phase++,gen); ``` Note gemv_conv writes to float y (P_Q). But P_Q is fp32 scratch. Good. But gemv_conv's cwin is bf16* (cq/ck/cv state buffers, bf16). Good. conv output y[out]=co (fp32, into P_Q fp32). Good. But wait — gemv_conv reads cwin (bf16 state) and convw (bf16). Writes y (fp32). And shifts cwin (bf16). Good. The q/k/v stored in P_Q/P_K/P_V are fp32 (the conv'd values). kda_recur reads them as float*. Good (q,k,v are conv'd outputs, fp32). g is fp32 (P_G, raw g_proj). beta fp32 (P_BETA). o fp32 (P_O). For o_proj: load_fold(sx, P_O (fp32), NULL, C). gemv_res reads sx. Good. Now MLA(b=3, HIN=h1, HOUT=h0): ``` // A: q_proj + kv_a load_fold(sx,(float*)P[P_H1],BF+BF_AN3,HID,red); gemv_om(sx,WQ+WQ_MQ,SC+SC_MQ,ZS+ZS_MQ,HID,H*(QK_NOPE+QK_ROPE),(float*)P[P_QFULL],bid); __syncthreads(); gemv_om(sx,WQ+WQ_MVA,SC+SC_MVA,ZS+ZS_MVA,HID,KV_LORA+QK_ROPE,(float*)P[P_KVFULL],bid); gbar(cnt,phase++,gen); // B: rope + append + q_absorb mla_rope_append((float*)P[P_QFULL],(float*)P[P_KVFULL],(bf16*)P[P_CKV],(bf16*)P[P_KRC],L,bid); __syncthreads(); mla_qabsorb((float*)P[P_QFULL],(uint8_t*)P[P_KVBUK_WQ],(bf16*)P[P_KVBUK_SC],(bf16*)P[P_KVBUK_ZR],(float*)P[P_QABS],bid); gbar(cnt,phase++,gen); // C: scores mla_scores((float*)P[P_QFULL],(float*)P[P_QABS],(bf16*)P[P_CKV],(bf16*)P[P_KRC],(float*)P[P_SCORES],L,bid); gbar(cnt,phase++,gen); // D: softmax mla_softmax((float*)P[P_SCORES],(float*)P[P_P],L,bid,smem,red); gbar(cnt,phase++,gen); // E: v_accum mla_vaccum((float*)P[P_P],(bf16*)P[P_CKV],(float*)P[P_VACC],L,bid); gbar(cnt,phase++,gen); // F: o mla_o((float*)P[P_VACC],(uint8_t*)P[P_KVBUV_WQ],(bf16*)P[P_KVBUV_SC],(bf16*)P[P_KVBUV_ZR],(float*)P[P_OFULL],bid); gbar(cnt,phase++,gen); // G: o_proj + residual -> h0 load_fold(sx,(float*)P[P_OFULL],(bf16*)0,H*VHD,red); gemv_res(sx,WQ+WQ_MO,SC+SC_MO,ZS+ZS_MO,H*VHD,HID,(float*)P[P_H0],bid,(float*)P[P_H1]); gbar(cnt,phase++,gen); // H: moe router + shared gateup (block 3) load_fold(sx,(float*)P[P_H0],BF+BF_MN3,HID,red); gemv_bf(sx,BF+BF_ROUT3,HID,64,(float*)P[P_ROUT],bid); __syncthreads(); gemv_gateup(sx,WQ+WQ_B3SGATE,...,(float*)P[P_HH]+8*M,bid); gbar(cnt,phase++,gen); // I: topk + routed gateup moe_topk(...); moe_routed_gateup(sx,WQ+WQ_B3GATE,...); gbar(cnt,phase++,gen); // J: down + residual -> h0 moe_down_resid(sx,WQ+WQ_B3DOWN,...,(float*)P[P_H1],(float*)P[P_H0],bid); gbar(cnt,phase++,gen); ``` Wait MLA block h_in=h1, h_out=h0. Let me write the dq_dot helper for down: ``` __device__ inline float dq_dot(const float* x,int K,const uint8_t* wq_row,const bf16* sc_row,const bf16* zr_row){ int lane=threadIdx.x&31; float acc=0; int ng=K/GROUP; // wq_row [K/2 bytes], sc_row[ng], zr_row[ng]. lane reads bytes stride 32. for(int b=lane;b<K/2;b+=32){ int g=b/64; float s=b2f(sc_row[g]),z=b2f(zr_row[g]); u32 by=wq_row[b]; int k0=2*b; acc += x[k0]*(((float)(by&0xF)-z)*s) + x[k0+1]*(((float)((by>>4)&0xF)-z)*s); } return wsum(acc); } ``` Wait, group g for byte b: k0=2b, g=k0/128=b/64. Correct (group=128 k = 64 bytes). sc_row[g]. Good. This dq_dot reads wq_row[b] for b=lane,lane+32 (stride 32 → coalesced). x[k0],x[k0+1] from... x must be in shared (sx). For down, x=hh[j] (fp32 global). Hmm, dq_dot reads x[k0] — if x is global (hh), strided reads. Let me load hh[j] to shared first? hh[j] is 1024 floats = 4KB. For the down phase, each warp c loops j (9 slots), each needing hh[j]. Loading hh[j] to shared per j is costly. Alternatively, dq_dot reads hh[j][k0] from global — for warp c, all 32 lanes read hh[j][lane*2..] → coalesced (32 consecutive). Actually x[k0] for k0=2*lane,2*(lane+32),... → lane reads hh[j][2*lane] and hh[j][2*lane+1]. 32 lanes → hh[j][0..63] (coalesced 64 floats). Then next b iteration hh[j][64..127]. So coalesced reads of hh[j]! Good (hh in global, coalesced). So dq_dot with x=hh[j] (global) reads coalesced. Good. No need to load hh to shared. But the down phase warp c loops j=0..8, each calling dq_dot(hh[j], M, down_wq[e][c], ...). The down_wq[e][c] = wq + e*HID*(M/2) + c*(M/2). For warp c, this row is M/2=512 bytes. Read by dq_dot (coalesced). OK. Let me write moe_down_resid: ``` __device__ inline void moe_down_resid(const uint8_t* dwq,const bf16* dsc,const bf16* dzr, const uint8_t* sdwq,const bf16* sdsc,const bf16* sdzr, float* hh, float* idxw, const float* hin, float* hout, int bid){ int tid=threadIdx.x,warp=tid>>5,lane=tid&31,gw=bid*WARPS+warp,TW=gridDim.x*WARPS; int stride_e = HID*(M/2); // down out-major per expert: [HID, M/2] for(int c=gw;c<HID;c+=TW){ float acc=0; for(int j=0;j<8;j++){ int e=(int)idxw[j]; float wj=idxw[8+j]; const uint8_t* row=dwq + (size_t)e*stride_e + (size_t)c*(M/2); const bf16* sr=dsc+(size_t)e*HID*(M/GROUP)+c*(M/GROUP); const bf16* zr=dzr+(size_t)e*HID*(M/GROUP)+c*(M/GROUP); acc += wj*dq_dot(hh+j*M, M, row, sr, zr); } // shared expert (slot 8) { const uint8_t* row=sdwq + (size_t)c*(M/2); const bf16* sr=sdsc+(size_t)c*(M/GROUP); const bf16* zr=sdzr+(size_t)c*(M/GROUP); acc += dq_dot(hh+8*M, M, row, sr, zr); } if(lane==0) hout[c]=hin[c]+acc; } } ``` Wait dq_dot returns wsum(acc) which is broadcast to all lanes (wsum makes all lanes have the sum). So acc (after dq_dot) is the full dot on all lanes. Then acc += wj*dot on all lanes (redundant but fine). After the j loop, all lanes have acc. lane==0 writes. Actually since wsum broadcasts, all lanes compute the same acc — wasteful (all 32 lanes do the j loop). But correct. To optimize, only lane 0 could do it, but dq_dot needs all 32 lanes for the dot. So all lanes participate in dq_dot (necessary), get the sum (broadcast), then the accumulate acc+=wj*dot is redundant across lanes but cheap. Fine. The down scales dsc: [64, HID, ng=8] out-major (e, c, g). offset e*HID*8 + c*8 + g. M/GROUP = 1024/128=8. So dsc+e*HID*8+c*8. Correct. OK. Let me write the full file now. I'll write it all. This is the definitive version. Let me also double check the conv: reference _short_conv: ``` win = cat([prev, val[None]], 0) # [4, C]: prev[0],prev[1],prev[2], val w = conv_w[idx].float().transpose(0,1) # [C, 4] out = (win.float()*w).sum(0) # [C] return silu(out), win[1:] # new prev = [prev[1], prev[2], val] ``` So out[c] = win[0,c]*w[c,0] + win[1,c]*w[c,1] + win[2,c]*w[c,2] + win[3,c]*w[c,3] = prev[0]*w0 + prev[1]*w1 + prev[2]*w2 + val*w3. Correct! And silu. new cwin: cwin[0]=p1=prev[1], cwin[1]=p2=prev[2], cwin[2]=acc=val. So new prev = [prev[1], prev[2], val]. For experts e>1, else branch, transpose(1,2). Fine. Hmm. Let me check if maybe the issue is that `put` is called and `self._wt[name]` doesn't exist for some name? No, _build_wt sets all. The model is W4A16: the big projection weights are stored as 4-bit integers, the activations and accumulation are bf16. reference.py is the correctness oracle and baseline.py is an already-optimized pure-PyTorch version; your job is to go beyond baseline.py. Your answer goes in solution.py and must expose the same Model class and the same step(hidden, state) -> (hidden, state) interface as reference.py, with identical buffer/parameter names so it loads the reference weights. Make whatever mess you want in this directory otherwise.  The unit is one repeating motif of Kimi-Linear-48B-A3B: four layers in the pattern [KDA, KDA, KDA, MLA], each followed by a 64-expert MoE FFN (top-8 routed + 1 shared, SiLU, intermediate 1024), with RMSNorm and residuals. Hidden size is 2304. The KDA layers are gated-delta linear attention with 32 heads of dim 128, a short causal depthwise conv (kernel 4) on q/k/v, a per-channel log-decay gate, and a per-head write strength; they carry a fixed-size recurrent state S[32,128,128] plus the conv window across decode steps. The MLA layers are multi-head latent attention with 32 heads, a kv_lora_rank of 512, qk_nope 128, qk_rope 64, v_head 128, decoupled RoPE (theta 10000); they carry a growing compressed latent KV cache (the 512-d latent plus the 64-d rope key per past token).  The weights are quantized W4A16, AWQ/GPTQ-style: per linear, the (in, out) weight is stored as int4 packed two-per-byte along `in` (w_q, uint8, shape (in//2, out)), with p
...

  32916       08:42 timeout 0 codex exec -m gpt-5.6-sol -c model_reasoning_effort="xhigh" --dangerously-bypass-approvals-and-sandbox --skip-git-repo-check -C /home/shadeform/kb-hard/outputs/runs/20260721_142442_codex_gpt-5.6-sol_02_kda_cutlass/repo/problems/02_kda_cutlass I need you to implement Kimi Delta Attention forward (chunk form) for the H100 PCIe (SM90 Hopper, HBM2e, 2.0 TB/s). The reference implementation is in reference.py and your kernel goes in solution.py. You can make whatever mess you want in this directory ??? scratch files, intermediate kernels, build artifacts, profiling traces ??? but the final answer has to be in solution.py with the same Model, get_inputs, and get_init_inputs interface as reference.py.  The op is the chunk-parallel KDA forward from the FLA library: q and k of shape (B, T, H, K) in bf16, v of shape (B, T, H, V) in bf16, g of shape (B, T, H, K) in fp32 (per-channel log-decay with in-chunk cumsum already applied), beta of shape (B, T, H) in bf16, scale a python float, chunk_size 64, no initial state, no final state. You return o of shape (B, T, H, V) in bf16. Correctness tolerance is 0.05 abs/rel ??? the long recurrence accumulates more error than a single GEMM so the bar's a bit looser than default bf16. The shapes you have to handle are B=2 T=1024 H=8 K=128 V=128 (short-context training step), B=2 T=2048 H=8 K=128 V=128 (the headline shape from the Kimi Linear paper), B=1 T=4096 H=8 K=128 V=128 (long context that stresses the inter-chunk recurrence), and B=1 T=2048 H=4 K=128 V=128 (thin batch decode).  This needs to be a real custom kernel ??? the whole point of the problem is to write the chunk-parallel attention yourself, not call FLA's existing implementation. Don't import or call fla.ops.kda, fla.ops.chunk_kda, chunk_kda, fused_recurrent_kda, naive_chunk_kda, or naive_recurrent_kda. The intended path is CUTLASS CuTe on SM90 but Triton, CUDA C++ via load_inline, or inline PTX are also fine if you prefer. Anything you're uncertain about, look up PTX docs, clone CUTLASS or FLA or other reference repos, read library headers, and investigate.  Your flywheel is implement, profile (ncu, nsys, torch.profiler ??? whatever's useful) and time it with benchmark.py, verify correctness by running `python check.py` and reading the output, then iterate. Don't substitute your own one-off correctness snippets for check.py ??? it iterates over every shape, your spot-check almost certainly won't. If `python check.py` hasn't printed PASS, you're not done. Take as long as you need to actually push the number up. This is single-query decode: each batch element has a query of shape (num_heads, head_dim) and attends over a KV cache of seq_len[b] tokens stored as fixed-size pages in a global pool, with block_table[b] listing which pages belong to batch element b. The KV cache is laid out as (num_blocks, page_size, num_kv_heads, head_dim * 2) where the last dim packs [K | V] so a single gather pulls both. You return the bf16 output of shape (batch, num_heads, head_dim). Correctness tolerance is 0.02 abs/rel on bf16/fp16. The shapes you have to handle are batch=8 num_heads=32 num_kv_heads=8 head_dim=128 seq_len=1024 page_size=16 (Llama-3 8B-style decode), batch=32 num_heads=32 num_kv_heads=8 head_dim=128 seq_len=2048 page_size=16 (server-batched decode), batch=4 num_heads=64 num_kv_heads=8 head_dim=128 seq_len=4096 page_size=16 (Llama-3 70B GQA wide ratio with long context), batch=16 num_heads=32 num_kv_heads=8 head_dim=128 seq_len=1535 page_size=16 (non-power-of-two seq_len that forces predicated tails), and batch=8 num_heads=16 num_kv_heads=4 head_dim=64 seq_len=2000 page_size=16 (small head_dim, non-pow2).  This needs to be a real custom kernel. Don't import or call vllm.attention, flashinfer.batch_decode_with_paged_kv_cache, flashinfer.decode, torch.nn.functional.scaled_dot_product_attention, or F.scaled_dot_product_attention ??? they're off-limits, including the trick of gathering pages first and then dispatching to SDPA. Try CUDA C++ via load_inline, Triton, inline PTX, or CUTLASS ??? whatever fits. Anything you're uncertain about, look up PTX docs, clone CUTLASS or vLLM or FlashInfer or other reference repos, read library headers, and investigate.  Your flywheel is implement, profile (ncu, nsys, torch.profiler ??? whatever's useful) and time it with benchmark.py, verify correctness by running `python check.py` and reading the output, then iterate. Don't substitute your own one-off correctness snippets for check.py ??? it iterates over every shape, your spot-check almost certainly won't. If `python check.py` hasn't printed PASS, you're not done. Take as long as you need to actually push the number up.   36465       00:39 /home/shadeform/mega/.venv/bin/python -c  import torch, time, solution, reference as R, baseline as B from math import exp, log cfg = R.build_config({'n_experts':64}) ref = R.Model(cfg).cuda().eval() sol = solution.Model(cfg).cuda().eval() base = B.Model(cfg).cuda().eval() sol.load_state_dict(ref.state_dict(), strict=True) base.load_state_dict(ref.state_dict(), strict=True) h0 = R.init_token(cfg, 0) for ctx in [64, 2048]:   st_r = R.init_state(cfg, ctx, 0); st_s = R.init_state(cfg, ctx, 0)   with torch.no_grad():     o_r,_=ref.step(h0.clone(), st_r); o_s,_=sol.step(h0.clone(), st_s)   cos=torch.nn.functional.cosine_similarity(o_r.float().flatten(), o_s.float().flatten(), dim=0)   print('cos', ctx, float(cos), 'fin', torch.isfinite(o_s).all().item(), flush=True)  def tm(m, ctx, steps=6):   with torch.no_grad():     for _ in range(2):       st=R.init_state(cfg,ctx,7); m.step(h0.clone(), st)     torch.cuda.synchronize()     t0=time.perf_counter()     for _ in range(steps):       st=R.init_state(cfg,ctx,7); m.step(h0.clone(), st)     torch.cuda.synchronize()   return (time.perf_counter()-t0)*1e3/steps sps=[] for ctx in [2048, 8192, 16384]:   ts, tb = tm(sol,ctx), tm(base,ctx,steps=3)   sp=tb/ts; sps.append(sp)   print(f'ctx={ctx}: sol {ts:.2f} base {tb:.2f} sp {sp:.2f}x', flush=True) print('gmean', exp(sum(log(s) for s in sps)/3), flush=True)

My layout treats e=1 as "2D transpose", but these are 3D with n=1. Fix: in put(), check the actual ndim, not e. If wqt.dim()==2: .t().contiguous(). Else (3D): .transpose(1,2).contiguous() (works for n=1 or n=64). Let me fix put to branch on ndim. Also the timing: "loaded 90.9s" — the lock contention added 90s. And import 0.0s (cached compile). Let me reconsider potential bugs in the kernel: 1. **load_fold with fp32 input but the buffer might not be fp32.** The KDA scratch _q etc. are fp32. h0/h1 fp32. qfull/kvfull/ofull fp32. So load_fold reads fp32 correctly. But the INPUT hidden (P_HIDDEN) is bf16, and phase 0 loads it via a separate bf16→fp32 copy (not load_fold). Good. And load_fold for block 0 reads h0 (fp32). Good. 2. **The norm folding**: load_fold computes sumsq of x (fp32), rsqrt(ss/HID+EPS), multiplies by wnorm. Reference: `_rmsnorm(x,w): xf=x.float(); xf=xf*rsqrt(xf.pow(2).mean(-1)+EPS); return (xf*w.float())`. So rmsnorm = x * rsqrt(mean(x^2)+EPS) * w. mean(x^2) = sum(x^2)/HID. So rsqrt(sum/HID + EPS). My code: rss=rsqrt(ss/HID+EPS). Matches. And sx[i]=x[i]*wnorm[i]*rss. Matches (x*w*rsqrt). Good. But wait — reference applies rsqrt to mean+EPS, i.e., rsqrt(sum/HID + EPS). My code: rsqrtf(ss/HID + EPS). ss=sum. Same. Good. 3. **gemv_om**: validated standalone (cos 1.0). But in the megakernel, the weight offset might be wrong. Let me double check the offset for K0Q. _build_layout: K0Q is first, offset 0. WQ_K0Q=0. The put() fills wq[0:...] with K0Q's transposed weight. Should match. But let me verify the transpose direction. For QuantLinear q_proj [in//2=1152, out=4096], .t() → [4096, 1152] (out, in//2). The gemv_om expects wq[N, K/2] = [out, in//2]. So [4096, 1152]. Matches. scales [ng=18, out=4096] → .t() → [4096, 18]. gemv expects sc[N, ng] = [out, ng] = [4096, 18]. Matches. Good. 4. **The barrier**: gbar uses gridDim.x. The kernel launched with G=numSM*mb. Should be fine. 5. **Phase 0 hidden load**: h0[i]=b2f(hidden[i]). hidden is the input bf16. Good. Then stop==0 dumps h0. So stop=0 output should equal input (cos 1.0). Let me see if that passes (tests the load+dump + pointer for hidden/out). 6. **KDA conv**: gemv_conv. The conv weight BF+BF_CONV0+idx*C*4. conv_w stored [3, C, 4]. BF_CONV0 offset, then idx*C*4 selects the idx slab, then out*4+t. conv_w[idx][c][t] = offset idx*C*4 + c*4 + t. My convw = BF+BF_CONV0+idx*C*4, convw[out*4+t]. Matches. But the conv_w is stored as [3, C, 4] in the model (Parameter shape [3, C, short_conv=4]). putbf flattens it view(-1) → order [idx, c, t]. So BF+BF_CONV0 + idx*(C*4) + c*4 + t. Correct. 7. **kda_recur**: reads q,k,v,g (fp32), S (fp32 state). Let me re-examine. q[idx]=q[h*DK+d] is the CONV'd q (stored in P_Q after gemv_conv). scalef=rsqrt(DK). shq[d]=q[idx]*scalef. Reference: q=q.view(H,Dk)*scale. So q*scale. My shq=q*scale. Good. But wait — q in reference is the conv'd q (after short_conv), and scale applied. My P_Q holds the conv'd q (gemv_conv writes conv'd value). Good. decay: shd[d]=1/(1+exp(g[idx])). g[idx] is raw g_proj. Reference decay=exp(-softplus(g_raw))=1/(1+exp(g_raw)). So 1/(1+exp(g_raw)). My code: 1/(1+__expf(g[idx])). Matches. beta: bh=1/(1+exp(-beta[h])). beta[h] raw beta_proj. Reference beta=sigmoid(beta_proj)=1/(1+exp(-raw)). Matches. The recurrent math (decay, pred, update, o) — I verified earlier. 8. **o_proj + residual**: gemv_res writes hout=acc+xres. xres=h_in. Good. 9. **MLA**: many phases. Likely bug source. But block 0 is KDA — if block 0 is wrong, I'll see it at stop=1. Let me wait for the debug output. Let me check the background task. Actually, let me also reconsider: the `put()` for experts. For B0GATE (QuantExperts [64, HID//2, M]), wqt.dim()==3 → transpose(1,2) → [64, M, HID//2]. The offset WQ_B0GATE = e*(in//2)*out = 64*(HID//2)*M = 64*1152*1024. The flat buffer stores [64, M, HID//2] flattened. gemv_routed_gateup accesses gwq + e*strideg + m*(HID/2) where strideg=M*(HID/2). So expert e at e*M*(HID/2), output m at m*(HID/2). The layout [64, M, HID//2] → expert e, output m, byte b at e*M*(HID/2)+m*(HID/2)+b. Matches strideg=M*(HID/2). Good. scales for experts: [64, ng, M] → transpose(1,2) → [64, M, ng]. offset SC_B0GATE = 64*ng*M. gemv accesses gsc + e*M*ng + m*ng. Layout [64,M,ng] → e*M*ng+m*ng+g. Matches. Good. +---
+name: env-shared-gpu-lock
+description: How to run Python/GPU jobs in the kimi_linear_decode workspace (shared H100, gpu.lock, uv, load_inline)
+metadata:
+  type: project
+---
+
+Workspace: `.../runs/20260721_125907_zai-claude_glm-5.2_02_kimi_linear_decode/repo/problems/02_kimi_linear_decode`.
+
+- **GPU is shared** by many concurrent agents (grok/codex/opus/etc.) via a flock at
+  `/home/shadeform/mega/outputs/gpu.lock` (`KBH_GPU_LOCK_WAIT_TIMEOUT_SECONDS=7200`).
+  Runs serialize behind it; a plain `timeout 90` often just waits on the lock and gets
+  killed, orphaning a python child that then holds the lock. Use long timeouts

Block output (h0) = h1 + attn + moe. ✓ Now parity: block0 hin=h0 hout=h1. block1 hin=h1 hout=h0. block2 hin=h0 hout=h1. block3(MLA) hin=h1 hout=h0. Let me reconsider the conv window update and the recurrent reading the RIGHT q. phase A: gemv_conv writes P_Q (conv'd q). Then phase B recur reads P_Q as q (conv'd). ✓. But gemv_conv ALSO updates cwin (cq) — shifts and stores RAW q (acc before conv). So cq now holds [prev1, prev2, raw_q_this_step]. Next step uses it. ✓. But wait — there's a subtle issue. gemv_conv computes acc (the raw projection q[c]), then conv_out = silu(...), writes y[out]=conv_out (to P_Q). And shifts cwin: cwin[2]=f2b(acc) (raw q). So P_Q holds conv_out (what recur reads as q). ✓. And cwin holds raw q's. ✓. Reference: q_proj output (raw) → short_conv → silu → q (what's used in recur). And the window stores raw q_proj outputs. ✓. OK. Let me reconsider the MLA q_absorb layout once more. Actually, let me reconsider whether q_absorb's qa indexing in mla_scores is right. qa[out] where out=h*KV_LORA+i (since q_absorb loops out=0..KV_LORA*H, h=out/KV_LORA, i=out%KV_LORA, writes qa[out]=qa[i*? no, qa[out] = qa[h*KV_LORA+i]? Let me fix. q_absorb writes qa[out] = qa[h*KV_LORA + i] (h-major). mla_scores reads qa[i*H+h] (i-major). These differ. Let me make them consistent. In mla_scores, scores[l,h] = sum_i c_kv[l,i]*qa[i,h]. The natural layout for qa is [i, h] (i-major) so that lane h reads qa[i*H+h] (consecutive h). Or [h, i] (h-major). q_absorb computes qa[i,h] for output (i,h). If I store qa[i*H+h] (i-major), then mla_scores lane h reads qa[i*H+lane] (consecutive). Let me make q_absorb write qa[i*H+h]. In q_absorb, out=h*KV_LORA+i currently → change to out=i*H+h. So `int out = i*H + h`. Let me restructure: loop out=0..KV_LORA*H, h=out%H, i=out/H? Or loop (i,h) and write qa[i*H+h]. Let me rewrite q_absorb to use out=i*H+h: in the loop, `int i=out/H, h=out%H`. Then qa[out]=qa[i*H+h]. mla_scores reads qa[i*H+lane]. Consistent. Let me fix q_absorb: change `int h=out/KV_LORA, i=out%KV_LORA` to `int i=out/H, h=out%H`. And the rest (h, i usage) stays. qa[out]=acc writes qa[i*H+h]. Let me make that fix. Also, let me double check mla_scores rope part: `qr=qfull+lane*(QK_NOPE+QK_ROPE)+QK_NOPE`. lane=head h. qfull[h*192+128] = q_rope for head h (after rope applied in phase B). qr[d]=qfull[h*192+128+d]. ✓. And krl=krc[l*64+...] (the rope cache). ✓. So the qa layout bug: fix q_absorb to write qa[i*H+h]. Let me edit. Let me also re-examine: in q_absorb, after fixing, qa has 16384 entries (KV_LORA*H=512*32). Layout [i, h] i-major. mla_scores reads qa[i*H+h]. ✓. Let me make the edit to q_absorb (line 356-358 area): change the h/i decomposition. So vh[i]=vacc[h*KV_LORA+i]. ✓ consistent (both h-major). vaccum: vacc[h*KV_LORA+ibase+il] += pl[h]*ckl[ibase+il]. pl=p+l*H, pl[h]=p[l*H+h] (h-major p). ckl[ibase+il]=c_kv[l*KV_LORA+ibase+il]. ✓. So vaccum and mla_o are consistent. Good. Only q_absorb/scores had the mismatch (now fixed). Now let me reconsider the scores indexing. mla_scores writes scores[l*H+lane]. mla_softmax reads scores[l*H+h]. ✓. p[l*H+h]. mla_vaccum reads pl[h]=p[l*H+h]. ✓. Good. Let me also double-check: mla_scores `nope += cv*qa[i*H+lane]`. qa now i-major [i*H+h]. lane=h. ✓. Now, let me reconsider potential issues with the recurrent and the scale. Actually wait — let me reconsider the KDA scale. Reference: `self.scale = Dk ** -0.5` applied to q: `q = q.view(H, Dk).float() * self.scale`. In recur, shq[d]=q[idx]*scalef where scalef=rsqrt(DK). ✓. And MLA scale = (qk_nope+qk_rope)**-0.5 = 192^-0.5 applied to scores. mla_scores: `sc=(nope+rp)*scalef`, scalef=rsqrt(192). ✓. Now let me reconsider a subtle issue with the **first step prime copy and the cache length**. On the first step (prime=1), the kernel copies ckv_in[:L*KV_LORA] → bigbuf, then appends row L. New length L+1. The scores loop l=0..L (Lp=L+1). ✓. But wait — `mla_rope_append_qabs` appends row L: `ckv[L*KV_LORA+i]=f2b(kvfull[i])`. And the prime copy copies ckv_in[0..L*KV_LORA-1] → ckv[0..L*KV_LORA-1]. So rows 0..L-1 from ckv_in, row L from kvfull. ✓. Total L+1 rows. ✓. After step, state[3]["c_kv"] = bigbuf[:L+1]. ✓. For the SECOND step (prime=0, since bigbuf exists and cap≥L+1): L is now the incoming c_kv length. But the incoming c_kv is bigbuf[:L_old+1] from step 1 (L=L_old+1). So L_new = L_old+1. The kernel appends row L_new. prime=0 (no copy, bigbuf already has rows 0..L_new-1 from step 1). Append row L_new. ✓. Wait — but on step 2, the incoming state[3]["c_kv"] is bigbuf[:L_old+1] (a VIEW into bigbuf). And ckv_in = state[3]["c_kv"] = bigbuf[:L_old+1]. So P_CKVIN = bigbuf (same buffer!). And prime=0, so no copy. The kernel appends row L_new=L_old+1 to bigbuf. ✓. And ckv (P_CKV) = bigbuf. ✓. Good, consistent. Hmm, but `prime = self._ckv_buf is None or self._ckv_buf.shape[0] < L + 1`. On step 2, L = L_old+1, cap = L_old+64 ≥ L+1. So prime=0. ✓. OK. Let me also reconsider: the `L` passed to the kernel = ckv_in.shape[0]. For step 1, ckv_in = reference init_state c_kv [ctx, 512]. L=ctx. For step 2, ckv_in = bigbuf[:ctx+1]. L=ctx+1. ✓. Now, one more potential issue: the **scores/p buffer size**. Allocated (LMAX_ALLOC+64)*H = (32768+64)*32. For ctx up to 16384 + 32 steps = 16416 < 32768. ✓. Now let me reconsider the barrier count and the `cnt` array size. I allocated cnt[128]. Let me count phases: - phase 0 (load hidden): 1 gbar. - Each KDA block: 6 gbars (A, B, C, D, E, F). ×3 = 18. - MLA block: A, B, C, D, D2(zero vacc), E, F, G, H, I, J = 11 gbars. Total = 1 + 18 + 11 = 30 phases. cnt[128] ≥ 30. ✓. Wait, let me recount MLA: phase A (1 gbar), B (1), C (1), D softmax (1), zero-vacc (1), E vaccum (1), F o (1), G o_proj resid (1), H router+sgateup (1), I topk+routed (1), J down resid (1) = 11 gbars. Plus the KDA blocks 6 each = 18. Plus phase 0 = 1. Total 30. cnt needs 30 entries. ✓ (128). Now, the `phase` variable increments per gbar. Max phase = 30. cnt[30] used. ✓. OK. Let me think about the MoE topk and the routed expert indexing once more. moe_topk: reads rout[64] (P_ROUT, the router output from gemv_bf). softmax, top-8, writes idxw_g[0..7]=idx, idxw_g[8..15]=w. moe_routed_gateup reads idxw[j] (e=expert), idxw[8+j] (w). ✓. moe_down_resid reads idxw[j], idxw[8+j]. ✓. But the router output (P_ROUT) — gemv_bf writes rout[out]=acc for out=0..63. Each block's warps cover 64 outputs (warp-per-output). With G*8 warps ≥ 64, each output written by one warp. But MULTIPLE blocks write the SAME rout[out] (since all blocks run gemv_bf covering all 64 outputs)? Let me check gemv_bf: `for(out=gw; out<N; out+=TW)` with gw=bid*8+warp, TW=G*8. For N=64, if TW≥64, each output written by ONE warp (gw maps uniquely). If TW<64, multiple warps... no, with round-robin, out=gw, gw+TW, ... For N=64 and TW=G*8 (say 912), out=gw only (gw<64 for ~64 warps, rest gw≥64 skip). So each output written once. ✓. But if TW>64, some warps have gw≥64 and skip. The 64 warps with gw<64 each write one output. ✓. No race. But wait — ALL blocks run gemv_bf. Block 0's warp 0 (gw=0) writes rout[0]. Block 1's warp 0 (gw=8) writes rout[8]. Etc. So different blocks write different outputs (gw=bid*8+warp, unique per global warp). With G*8 ≥ 64, the first 64 global warps (across blocks) write rout[0..63]. Each output once. ✓. No race. Good. Hmm, but actually if G*8 > 64, then global warps 0..63 write rout[0..63], warps 64+ skip. Global warp gw belongs to block gw/8, warp gw%8. So blocks 0..7 contribute (8 warps each, 64 total). Each writes one rout. ✓. OK that's fine. Let me also reconsider: the shared expert gateup (gemv_gateup1) writes hh+8*M. All blocks run it (warp-per-output over M=1024). Each output m written by one global warp. ✓. routed_gateup: warp-per-(j,m), writes hh[j*M+m]. ✓. Now, the hh buffer: slots 0..7 (routed, from topk order), slot 8 (shared). down_resid: for j=0..7 uses hh[j*M] with expert idxw[j]; slot 8 uses hh[8*M] with shared. ✓. OK. Let me also reconsider gemv_gateup1 for shared: it reads sx (hn folded). The sx was loaded in phase D (load_fold hout, MN{b}). Then gemv_bf (router), __syncthreads, gemv_gateup1 (shared). All read sx. sx unchanged. ✓. Then phase E (topk + routed_gateup) — does it reload sx? NO. sx from phase D still valid (nothing overwrote it between D's gbar and E's use). Phase E: moe_topk (uses smem+KDA_SMEM, not sx), __syncthreads, moe_routed_gateup (reads sx). So sx (hn) still in shared from phase D. ✓. But wait — moe_topk uses smem+KDA_SMEM (=smem+4368). And sx is smem[0..4095]. No overlap. And the __syncthreads in moe_topk doesn't touch sx. So sx persists into phase E. ✓. OK I'm fairly confident the 3 bugs are the main issues. Let me kill and rerun. Actually, let me reconsider ONE more: the KDA phase A loads sx with load_fold(hin, AN{b}). Then gemv_conv(q), __syncthreads, gemv_conv(k), ..., gemv_om(g), __syncthreads, gemv_bf(beta). The __syncthreads between q and k: is it needed? gemv_conv writes P_Q (global) and cq (global). It reads sx (shared). The next gemv_conv(k) reads sx (unchanged). So the __syncthreads is just a sync (harmless). But does gemv_conv modify sx? No. So it's fine. But actually — gemv_conv's internal warp reduce and the `if(lane==0)` write: different warps write different out. No shared mem write in gemv_conv. So sx is read-only across the q/k/v/g/beta gemvs. The __syncthreads between them is unnecessary but harmless. ✓. OK let me kill and rerun dbg_blk.

Let me reconsider the barrier `want=(gen+1)*gridDim.x`. gen from Python (self._gen, increments per step). gridDim.x = G (numSM*mb). So want = (gen+1)*G. cnt[phase] accumulates atomicAdd by each block. After all G blocks, cnt[phase]=(gen+1)*G. ✓. But across steps, gen increments, so cnt[phase] grows: step0 → G, step1 → 2G, etc. The spin `while(cnt<want)` waits for the current step's threshold. ✓. And cnt never resets (monotonic). For 30 phases, cnt[0..29] used. ✓. Hmm, but there's a subtle issue: `gridDim.x` is the SAME for all steps (G constant). And gen increments. So want=(gen+1)*G. For step 0, want=G. cnt[phase] after step 0 = G (each of G blocks added 1). For step 1, want=2G. cnt[phase] needs to reach 2G (each block adds 1 again → G more → total 2G). ✓. Consistent. Good. But wait — the SAME cnt array is used across steps (kernel launches). Between launches, the host doesn't touch cnt. Each launch's barriers add to cnt. gen ensures monotonic thresholds. ✓. As long as G is the SAME each launch (it is, same kernel/config). ✓. OK. Let me wait for the debug output. Let me check periodically. Let me also reconsider: is there an issue with the `_stop` attribute and the full step? In step(), `getattr(self, "_stop", 4)`. For check.py/benchmark (which don't set _stop), default 4 → full step. ✓. My debug sets sm._stop=N. ✓. Let me wait and check the debug output. Actually, let me reconsider the mla_o more carefully — the kvb_UV_T extraction in Python. uv_wq = wqb.index_select(1, uv_cols).reshape(256, H, VHD).permute(1, 2, 0).contiguous() - wqb [256, 8192]. index_select(1, uv_cols) where uv_cols = [h*256+128+d for h,d] → [256, H*128]. - reshape(256, H, VHD=128) → [256, H, 128] (in//2, h, d). - permute(1,2,0) → [H, 128, 256]. So uv_wq[h][d][b] = the gathered value. uv_cols is ordered (h outer, d inner): uv_cols[h*128+d] = h*256+128+d. So index_select column j=h*128+d → wqb[:, h*256+128+d]. reshape(256, H, 128): the H*128 dim → [H, 128] = [h, d]. So [256, h, d]. wqb_col[h*128+d] → element [256, h, d]. permute(1,2,0) → [h, d, 256]. So uv_wq[h][d][b] = wqb[b, h*256+128+d]. mla_o: row=wq+h*VHD*(KV_LORA/2)+d*(KV_LORA/2) = h*128*256 + d*256. row[b] = uv_wq[h][d][b]. ✓. And that should = kv_b weight (i=2b or 2b+1, col=h*256+128+d). ✓. Good. uv_sc: scb.index_select(1, uv_cols).reshape(4, H, VHD).permute(1,2,0) → [H, 128, 4]. mla_o: sc+h*VHD*ng+d*ng = h*128*4+d*4. sc[g] = uv_sc[h][d][g]. ✓. OK. Let me wait for debug. The dbg5 reads sol._o (the recurrent OUTPUT), not S. So S update correctness doesn't affect _o comparison directly... except _o depends on the updated S. If S update is wrong, _o is wrong. Actually, _o is computed from the UPDATED S (step 4: o=sum_j S*q). If the update (step 3) or decay (step 1) is wrong, S is wrong, so o is wrong. cos 0.665 on o. Let me dump S after the step and compare to reference S. That isolates whether the decay/pred/update is wrong. Let me also dump pred. Actually, let me reconsider a SUBTLE bug: in the pred computation, `shp[d]` is accumulated by threads 0..127 (one per d). But the loop is `for(int j=0;j<DK;j++){ ...; __syncthreads();}`. The __syncthreads is INSIDE the loop. After the loop, shp[d] = pred[d]. Then the update uses shp. But WAIT — between the pred loop and the update loop, is there a __syncthreads? The pred loop's last iteration ends with __syncthreads. Then the update loop starts. The update reads shp[d] (pred) and shk[j], shv[d]. These are in shared (unchanged since load). ✓. And writes S (global). Hmm. Let me reconsider — actually, maybe the bug is that I'm reading S from GLOBAL but the decay multiply writes S, then pred reads S — but the decay write and pred read are different threads/iterations. The decay loop: `for(jd=tid;...)S[...]*=shd[j]`. All 256 threads write their S elements. Then __syncthreads. Then pred reads S. Since S is in GLOBAL memory, the __syncthreads does NOT guarantee global memory visibility across threads! __syncthreads only syncs within the block AND orders shared mem. For global memory, I need __threadfence() before the pred reads the decayed S written by other threads! THAT'S THE BUG. The decay loop writes S[h*...]*=decay (global). Different threads write different S elements. Then __syncthreads. Then pred reads S[h*...]. But __syncthreads does NOT flush global writes to a scope visible to other threads' reads (without a fence). Actually — __syncthreads DOES provide a memory fence for the block: "all memory writes from threads in the block are visible to all other threads in the block after the sync." Wait, does it? From CUDA docs: "__syncthreads() acts as a memory fence for shared memory and ensures that all memory writes (global AND shared) by threads in the block are visible to other threads in the block before any subsequent memory operations." Hmm, actually I'm not 100% sure __syncthreads fences global memory within the block. Let me reconsider. Actually, the CUDA programming guide says __syncthreads ensures data coherency for shared memory. For global memory, the ordering is: writes by a thread are visible to itself (program order), but visibility to OTHER threads requires a fence. __syncthreads on H100... Actually, __syncthreads does provide acquire/release semantics for the block such that prior global writes by any thread in the block are visible to all threads after the barrier. Let me verify: "In addition to the synchronization, __syncthreads also ensures that all writes to shared and global memory made by a thread before the call to __syncthreads are visible to all threads in the block after the call." Hmm, actually I recall __syncthreads does NOT officially guarantee global memory visibility — only shared. But in practice, on most architectures, the barrier includes a fence. To be SAFE, I should add __threadfence() or __threadfence_block() before the reads. But wait — if that were the issue, the values would be RANDOM (stale reads), giving cos ~0, not 0.665. cos 0.665 suggests MOSTLY right with a systematic component wrong. Actually, let me reconsider whether __syncthreads fences global. I recall now: __syncthreads provides a barrier with memory fence semantics for the BLOCK scope — it ensures that all memory accesses (global and shared) by threads in the block that occur BEFORE the __syncthreads are completed and visible to threads in the block AFTER the __syncthreads. This is documented. So cross-thread global visibility within the block IS guaranteed. BUT — there's a subtlety with the COMPILER. If the compiler doesn't know S is modified by other threads, it might cache S reads. The `S` pointer is `float*` (not volatile). The compiler might assume S[x] doesn't change across the __syncthreads (since it's not volatile and the compiler might not model cross-thread writes). This could cause the compiler to reuse a stale REGISTER value for S[x] if the same thread reads S[x] before and after. But in recur, thread T writes S[x] in decay, and reads S[y] (different y) in pred — different elements. So no register reuse of the same element. Hmm. Actually wait — let me reconsider. For thread T: decay writes S[T's elements]. pred reads S[d's elements] (different). The compiler emits: decay stores, __syncthreads, pred loads. The loads go to global (or L1/L2 cache). +import sys; sys.path.insert(0, ".")
+import torch, torch.nn.functional as F, reference as R, solution
+dev = torch.device("cuda:0")
+
+
+def cos(a, b):
+    return F.cosine_similarity(a.float().flatten(), b.float().flatten(), dim=0).item()
+
+
+for ctx in [2048, 8192]:
+    for seed in [0, 1, 2]:
+        cfg = R.build_config({"context_len": ctx, "n_experts": 64})
+        rm = R.Model(cfg).to(dev).eval()

But the incoming state[3]["c_kv"] is a FRESH init_state (not my bigbuf). So prime=0 means no copy of the incoming cache into bigbuf. The bigbuf still holds STALE data from the previous step (seed 0 ctx 8192). So the MLA attention uses stale cache → wrong output and wrong cache → cos 0.0423. This is the persistence bug. Actually — the contract: in benchmark/check, each "run" starts with a fresh init_state and does multiple AR steps. Across DIFFERENT runs (different seed/ctx), fresh state. Within a run, the state is threaded (my bigbuf view persists). So prime should be: True if incoming c_kv is NOT a view of my bigbuf. Detect via data_ptr comparison. But the incoming c_kv after my step is bigbuf[:L+1] (a view, same storage). So data_ptr(incoming) == bigbuf.data_ptr() (if it starts at offset 0). Let me check: state[3]["c_kv"] = self._ckv_buf[:L+1]. data_ptr of this view = bigbuf.data_ptr() (offset 0). So compare incoming.data_ptr() == self._ckv_buf.data_ptr(). The incoming shape[0] = L_prev+1 = current L. Actually, simplest robust detection: prime = NOT (incoming.data_ptr() == self._ckv_buf.data_ptr()). If the incoming cache IS my bigbuf (continued chain), prime=0. Else (fresh state OR reallocated), prime=1 and ensure bigbuf big enough. But there's a subtlety: in benchmark's _run, the AR loop does `h, st = model.step(h, st)`. So st is threaded. After step 1, st[3]["c_kv"] = my bigbuf view. Step 2: incoming = my bigbuf view → prime=0. ✓. And the FIRST step of each _run: incoming = fresh init_state → prime=1. ✓. And check.py: each (seed,ctx) is a single step with fresh state → prime=1 each time. ✓. But also: if bigbuf is too small for the new L (e.g., ctx grew), reallocate AND prime=1. Let me handle: if incoming is my bigbuf (continued), prime=0, bigbuf stays. If fresh state, prime=1, and ensure bigbuf sized (reallocate if needed). Wait — but if incoming is my bigbuf and L grew (continued AR chain, L increases by 1 each step), bigbuf must have room (cap ≥ L+1). I allocated cap = L_init + 64. After 64 steps it'd overflow. But benchmark does ≤32 steps. So cap = L_init+64 is enough for one _run. For the next _run (fresh state, possibly different ctx), prime=1, reallocate if needed. Let me fix the prime logic: ``` incoming = state[3]["c_kv"] L = incoming.shape[0] if self._ckv_buf is not None and incoming.data_ptr() == self._ckv_buf.data_ptr(): prime = 0 # continued AR chain, bigbuf already has the cache else: prime = 1 # fresh state cap = L + 64 if self._ckv_buf is None or self._ckv_buf.shape[0] < cap: self._ckv_buf = torch.zeros(cap, KV_LORA, ...) self._krc_buf = torch.zeros(cap, QK_ROPE, ...) ``` This fixes the persistence bug. Let me also double-check: on prime=0 (continued), the bigbuf already has rows 0..L-1 (from prev step) and we append row L. The incoming state[3]["c_kv"] = bigbuf[:L] (prev step set it to bigbuf[:L_prev+1] where L_prev+1 = L). So incoming.shape[0] = L. And bigbuf has rows 0..L-1 (= 0..L_prev). Append row L. ✓. Wait — prev step: L_prev incoming, appended row L_prev, set state c_kv = bigbuf[:L_prev+1]. @@ -0,0 +1,21 @@
+import sys, time; sys.path.insert(0, ".")
+import torch, reference as R, solution
+dev = torch.device("cuda:0")
+for ctx in [2048, 8192, 16384]:
+    cfg = R.build_config({"context_len": ctx, "n_experts": 64})
+    rm = R.Model(cfg).to(dev).eval(); sm = solution.Model(cfg).to(dev).eval()
+    sm.load_state_dict(rm.state_dict(), strict=True)
+    st = R.init_state(cfg, ctx, 7); h = R.init_token(cfg, 7)
+    # warmup (incl prime)
+    with torch.no_grad():
+        for _ in range(3): h, st = sm.step(h, st)
+    torch.cuda.synchronize()
+    # time N steps

The whole kernel is bandwidth-starved. Why? The persistent kernel with G blocks. For the GEMV phases, the bandwidth depends on how well the warps stream the weights. My gemv_om reads 1 byte per lane per iteration (uint8). That's inefficient — each thread issues a 1-byte load. The memory system wants wider loads (16-byte vectors). Also — the grid might be too small. Let me check G. Let me add a print. Actually, let me reconsider: if mb (maxBlocksPerSM) is small (say 1-2), G=114-228. For the MoE phases (need lots of parallelism), 228 blocks might underutilize. But the bigger issue is the 49 GB/s effective bandwidth. Let me reconsider the gemv access pattern. In gemv_om, lane reads `row[bi]` where bi=g*64+c*32+lane. So within a warp, lanes 0..31 read bytes bi=lane (c=0) → bytes g*64+0..31 (32 consecutive bytes, coalesced into 1-2 transactions). Then c=1: bytes g*64+32..63. So 2 transactions of 32 bytes each per group per warp. That's coalesced but NARROW (32-byte transactions). H100 prefers 128-byte transactions. Each byte load is 1 byte but coalesced into a 32-byte sector. So 32 lanes → 32 bytes → 1 sector (32B). The memory system transfers 32B minimum per sector. So 32 bytes useful per sector. That's OK-ish (no over-fetch). But the issue: each lane does MANY 1-byte loads (one per k-pair). For K=2304, 1152 byte-loads per output. With the loop unrolled 2 (c=0,1 per group), 18 groups × 2 = 36 byte-loads per output per lane. Each byte-load is a 1-byte read by 32 lanes (coalesced 32B). So 36 sectors per output. For N outputs... Hmm. The bandwidth efficiency: reading the weight (int4) should be 0.5 bytes/element. For q_proj [2304,4096] = 4.7MB int4. At 2TB/s → 2.4µs. My kernel reads it but slowly. Actually — let me reconsider. The standalone gemv test got 328 GB/s for K=2304 N=4096 (15µs for 5MB). But that was a SEPARATE kernel launch (no megakernel overhead). In the megakernel, the gemv is a phase. The phase should be similar speed IF the grid is large enough. Wait — but in the megakernel, the gemv phase uses G blocks (persistent grid), and the assignment is warp-per-output with round-robin. For N=4096 outputs and G*8 warps, if G*8 ≥ 4096, each warp does ~1 output. If G*8 < 4096, each warp does multiple. Let me check G. Let me add a host-side print of G. Actually — let me reconsider the smem. I set smem=(4096+256+2048)*4 = 25600 bytes = 25KB. With 25KB shared/block, H100 (232KB/SM) → up to 9 blocks/SM. But registers might limit. Let me query. Actually — the REAL issue might be that the megakernel has LOW occupancy because it's one giant kernel with many phases, and the compiler allocates lots of registers (for all the phase code), limiting mb to 1-2. Then G=114-228, and the parallel phases are underutilized. Let me check the actual occupancy/register usage. Let me look at the compile output (register count) or query at runtime. Let me add a query: in the launcher, print numSM, mb, G. Let me add a Python-accessible print. Actually, let me just reason: the kernel is 4.6ms at ctx=2048 for ~226MB weight = 49 GB/s. To hit ~500 GB/s (a reasonable target for this access pattern), I'd be at ~450µs. To hit 1TB/s, ~226µs. The biggest lever: increase bandwidth utilization. Options: 1. **Vectorize loads**: read uint32 (4 bytes) or uint64 (8 bytes) per thread instead of uint8. This issues fewer, wider loads. Each thread processes 8 or 16 k-values per load. 2. **Increase grid/occupancy**: ensure G is large enough. 3. **Reduce barrier overhead**. 4. **Fix the MLA O(L) parallelism** (v_accum, scores). Let me first check the occupancy and grid size, then vectorize the GEMV loads. Let me add a debug print of G via an environment-variable-gated print in the launcher. Let me instead query via a separate mechanism. Actually — let me just reason and optimize. Let me reconsider the whole performance picture. 4.6ms at ctx=2048. The work: - 3 KDA blocks: ~75MB weight + MLA block ~15MB + 4 MoE ~136MB = 226MB. - Plus MLA cache traffic at ctx=2048 (small). - Plus barriers (~30). At 49 GB/s, something is bottlenecking bandwidth severely. The most likely culprit: the gemv reads 1 byte at a time (uint8) with no vectorization, AND the grid/occupancy is low. Let me vectorize to uint32 (4 bytes = 8 int4 per load) and ensure good occupancy. Actually — let me reconsider an even more likely culprit: the **barrier**. 30 barriers. If each barrier is ~100µs (due to atomic serialization with G blocks + spin), that's 3ms! That would explain a lot. Let me reconsider the barrier cost. The barrier: thread 0 of each block does atomicAdd(cnt[phase], 1) then spins. With G blocks, G atomicAdds to cnt[phase]. These serialize at L2. G atomicAdds × ~30-50ns L2 latency = G*40ns. For G=456, that's 18µs per barrier just for the atomicAdds (serialized). Plus the spin. × 30 phases = 540µs. Hmm, that's 0.5ms, not 3ms. But wait — the spin! After atomicAdd, thread 0 spins `while(*c < want)`. The spin is a volatile load of `*c` in a tight loop. While spinning, it hammers L2. AND — the OTHER threads in the block (255 of them) are at the `__syncthreads()` AFTER the spin (waiting for thread 0). So 255 threads idle, thread 0 spins. The spin time = time until the slowest block finishes its phase work + arrives. For phases where work is balanced (all blocks do similar work), the slowest is ~the average. For phases where few blocks work (recur: 32, v_accum: 16, softmax: 1), the working blocks take longer, and ALL G blocks wait for them. So the barrier time = the working block's phase time. For the MoE phases (all blocks work), the barrier time = the phase compute time. Hmm. So the total kernel time ≈ sum of phase times (each phase = max block time + barrier overhead). The phase times are dominated by the GEMV bandwidth. If GEMV is at 49 GB/s, the phases are slow. Let me focus on GEMV bandwidth. Let me check occupancy first, then vectorize. Let me just add a printf to the launcher (temporary) to print G, numSM, mb, smem, regs. That requires recompile but is the clearest. Actually — let me reason about registers. The kernel has MANY device function calls (gemv_om, gemv_conv, kda_recur, mla_*, moe_*). The compiler might inline them all, using lots of registers. If reg usage > 64/thread, occupancy drops. With 256 threads/block and 64 regs → 256*64 = 16K regs/block. H100 has 65536 regs/SM → 4 blocks/SM (if 64 regs). If 128 regs → 2 blocks/SM. If the kernel uses 100+ regs, mb=1-2. +    int mb=1; cudaOccupancyMaxActiveBlocksPerMultiprocessor(&mb,mega,THREADS,smem);

218	__device__ inline void gemv_om(const float* sx,const uint8_t* wq,const bf16* sc,const bf16* zr,int K,int N,float* y,int bid){
219	    int tid=threadIdx.x,warp=tid>>5,lane=tid&31,ng=K/GROUP,gw=bid*WARPS+warp,TW=gridDim.x*WARPS;
220	    for(int out=gw;out<N;out+=TW){
221	        const uint8_t* row=wq+(size_t)out*(K/2); const bf16* s=sc+(size_t)out*ng; const bf16* z=zr+(size_t)out*ng;
222	        float acc=0;
223	        for(int g=0;g<ng;g++){float ss=b2f(s[g]),zz=b2f(z[g]);
224	            for(int c=0;c<2;c++){int bi=g*64+c*32+lane;u32 by=row[bi];int k0=2*bi;
225	                acc+=sx[k0]*(((float)(by&0xF)-zz)*ss)+sx[k0+1]*(((float)((by>>4)&0xF)-zz)*ss);}
226	        }
227	        acc=wsum(acc); if(lane==0) y[out]=acc;

The vectorized dot: ``` __device__ inline float gemv_dot_v(const float* sx, const uint8_t* wq_row, const bf16* sc, const bf16* zr, int K, int lane){ const uint32_t* r=(const uint32_t*)wq_row; int K8=K>>3; float acc=0; for(int j=lane;j<K8;j+=32){ uint32_t u=r[j]; int k0=j<<3; float ss=b2f(sc[j>>4]), zz=b2f(zr[j>>4]); const float* xb=sx+k0; #pragma unroll for(int b=0;b<4;b++){ uint32_t by=(u>>(b<<3))&0xFFu; acc += xb[b*2]*(((float)(by&0xF)-zz)*ss) + xb[b*2+1]*(((float)((by>>4)&0xF)-zz)*ss); } } return wsum(acc); } ``` j>>4 = j/16 = group (since k0=8j, group=k0/128=8j/128=j/16). ✓. gemv_om: ``` __device__ inline void gemv_om(const float* sx,const uint8_t* wq,const bf16* sc,const bf16* zr,int K,int N,float* y,int bid){ int tid=threadIdx.x,warp=tid>>5,lane=tid&31,ng=K/GROUP,gw=bid*WARPS+warp,TW=gridDim.x*WARPS; for(int out=gw;out<N;out+=TW){ float acc=gemv_dot_v(sx,wq+(size_t)out*(K/2),sc+(size_t)out*ng,zr+(size_t)out*ng,K,lane); if(lane==0) y[out]=acc; } } ``` Wait — gemv_dot_v returns wsum(acc) which is broadcast (all lanes have the dot). So the byte-version dq_dot reads hh COALESCED (x[2b], x[2b+1] for b=lane → consecutive). But the uint32 version reads x[k0..k0+7] for k0=8j, j=lane → hh[8*lane..8*lane+7] → stride 8 across lanes → uncoalesced. WORSE for global x. [Edit] /home/shadeform/mega/outputs/runs/20260721_125907_zai-claude_glm-5.2_02_kimi_linear_decode/repo/problems/02_kimi_linear_decode/solution.py
--- a//home/shadeform/mega/outputs/runs/20260721_125907_zai-claude_glm-5.2_02_kimi_linear_decode/repo/problems/02_kimi_linear_decode/solution.py
+++ b//home/shadeform/mega/outputs/runs/20260721_125907_zai-claude_glm-5.2_02_kimi_linear_decode/repo/problems/02_kimi_linear_decode/solution.py
@@ -1,26 +1,28 @@
+// vectorized dequant dot over one weight row (uint32 loads, 128B transactions).
+// Returns warp-reduced dot (broadcast to all lanes). wq_row aligned to 4 bytes (K%8==0).
+__device__ inline float gemv_dot_v(const float* x,const uint8_t* wq_row,const bf16* sc,const bf16* zr,int K,int lane){
+    const uint32_t* r=(const uint32_t*)wq_row; int K8=K>>3; float acc=0;
+    for(int j=lane;j<K8;j+=32){ uint32_t u=r[j]; int k0=j<<3; float ss=b2f(sc[j>>4]),zz=b2f(zr[j>>4]);
+        const float* xb=x+k0;
+        #pragma unroll
+        for(int b=0;b<4;b++){ uint32_t by=(u>>(b<<3))&0xFFu;
+            acc += xb[b*2]*(((float)(by&0xF)-zz)*ss) + xb[b*2+1]*(((float)((by>>4)&0xF)-zz)*ss);
+        }
+    }
+    return wsum(acc);
+}

Each phase, ALL 228 blocks work (or idle). The phase time = slowest block. For the GEMV phases, each block's warps cover some outputs. Actually — maybe the issue is that the kernel has a LONG serial chain of 30 phases, each with a barrier, and the barrier cost is HIGH. Let me estimate barrier cost. With G=228 blocks, each barrier: 228 atomicAdds (serialized at L2, ~40ns each = 9µs) + spin. × 30 phases = 270µs just in atomic serialization. Plus the spin while waiting for the slowest block. But 270µs << 4.5ms. So barriers aren't the main cost. Let me reconsider — maybe the issue is that the GEMV phases are genuinely slow due to the access pattern. Let me reconsider gemv_dot_v's shared memory access. xb[b*2], xb[b*2+1] for b=0..3 — 8 consecutive floats from sx. 32 lanes each read 8 floats at k0=8j, j=lane. From SHARED mem. Shared bank conflicts: 32 banks (4 bytes each). sx[k0+0] for lane = sx[8*lane]. banks = (8*lane)%32 = (8*lane)&31. lane 0→bank0, lane1→bank8, lane2→bank16, lane3→bank24, lane4→bank0 (conflict with lane0!). So 4-way bank conflict on each shared read. 8 reads × 4-way = effectively 32 serialized accesses. That SLOWS the shared reads significantly. Hmm. The shared bank conflict (4-way) on the x reads could be the issue. The weight reads (global, coalesced via uint32) are fine. But the shared x reads conflict. Actually — wait. Let me reconsider. The original byte-version gemv read sx[k0], sx[k0+1] for k0=2*bi, bi=g*64+c*32+lane. So sx[2*(g*64+c*32+lane)] = sx at stride 2 across lanes → banks (2*lane)&31, lane 0→0, lane1→2,... lane15→30, lane16→0 (2-way conflict). So 2-way conflict in the byte version. The uint32 version has 4-way conflict (worse shared). But the uint32 version has wider weight loads (better global). Net: marginal (which matches the ~2% improvement). So the shared bank conflicts and the global bandwidth roughly balance. Neither is the 10x bottleneck. Let me reconsider what's actually slow. 4.5ms. Let me PROFILE with ncu to find the hotspot. Or torch.profiler. Let me reconsider — maybe the issue is the MLA at ctx=2048 isn't the issue (small L), it's the MoE or KDA. Let me reconsider: at ctx=2048, MLA is small (L=2048, scores/v_accum ~moderate). The bulk is KDA (3 blocks) + MoE (4 blocks). 4.5ms for ~226MB weight. Let me check: is the kernel compute-bound? The MoE: 9 experts × 3 gemvs each, each ~2.36M params. Total MoE work ~64M MACs per block's worth... Actually the FLOPS: per expert gate/up: 2304*1024*2 = 4.7M MACs (gate+up). down: 1024*2304 = 2.36M. Per expert ~7M MACs. ×9 ×4 blocks = 252M MACs. Plus KDA/MLA projections. ~400M MACs total. At ~100 TFLOPS (bf16 FMA, but I'm using fp32 scalar FMA ~much less). Wait — my FMA is fp32 SCALAR (not tensor core). Each thread does 1 FMA/cycle. 228 blocks × 256 threads × 1.5GHz × 2 FMA (if dual-issue) ≈ 175 GFLOPS fp32. For 400M MACs = 800M FLOPs → 800M/175G = 4.6ms!! THAT'S IT. The kernel is COMPUTE-BOUND on scalar fp32 FMA, not bandwidth! 400M MACs / 175 GFLOPS = 2.3ms (just MoE), plus the rest. Total ~4.5ms. Matches! So the bottleneck is COMPUTE (scalar fp32 FMA), not bandwidth. My dequant-GEMV does scalar FMAs (one per element). For 400M+ MACs at ~175 GFLOPS scalar, it's slow. Wait — but I thought it was bandwidth-bound (the int4 weights are the point). Let me reconsider. The weight is 226MB int4. At 2TB/s → 110µs. The compute is 400M MACs. For a GEMV, each MAC reads ~0.5 bytes (int4) of weight. So 400M MACs × 0.5 bytes = 200MB reads. Bandwidth: 200MB/2TB/s = 100µs. Compute: 400M/175G = 2.3ms. So COMPUTE-bound by 23x! Hmm. But that contradicts the premise that batch-1 decode is bandwidth-bound. The premise: at batch 1, the weight is read ONCE per token (bandwidth), and the compute is minimal (M=1 → few FLOPs per weight byte). For int4, each weight byte (2 int4) contributes 2 MACs. So 2 MACs per byte. Arithmetic intensity = 2 MACs / 1 byte = 0.5 FLOP/byte. At 2TB/s bandwidth and 175 GFLOPS scalar, the balance point: 175G/2T = 0.0875 FLOP/byte. Since 0.5 > 0.0875, it's... compute bound? No wait. Arithmetic intensity AI = FLOPs/bytes = 2 MACs/byte = 2 FLOP/byte (1 MAC = 2 FLOP). Roofline: compute throughput / mem throughput = 175e12 / 2e12 = 87.5 FLOP/byte. If AI (2) < 87.5, it's BANDWIDTH bound. So it SHOULD be bandwidth bound. But my measurement says 4.5ms for 200MB = 44 GB/s (bandwidth) AND 400M MACs. If bandwidth-bound at 2TB/s, it'd be 100µs. At 44 GB/s, it's 10x slow. So I'm achieving only 44 GB/s, not 2TB/s. AND the compute (400M MACs) at 175 GFLOPS = 2.3ms. So actually both are ~consistent with 4.5ms being compute-bound (2.3ms MoE + rest). Wait, let me recompute the FLOPs. The dequant: for each weight element, 1 multiply (x*w) + accumulate. Plus the dequant overhead ((int-zero)*scale = 2 ops). So per weight element: ~4 FLOPs (dequant) + 2 (MAC) = 6 FLOPs per int4 element. For 400M weight elements (the 226MB / 0.5 byte... 226MB int4 = 452M int4 elements). × 6 FLOPs = 2.7 GFLOP. At 175 GFLOPS = 15ms?? That doesn't match either. Hmm, let me recompute. 226MB of int4 weights = 226MB / 0.5 bytes-per-int4 = 452M int4 elements. Each element: dequant (sub + mul = 2 FLOP) + MAC (mul + add = 2 FLOP) = 4 FLOP. Total 452M × 4 = 1.8 GFLOP. At 175 GFLOPS = 10ms. Plus scales/zeros loads. So ~10ms compute. But measured 4.5ms. So I'm achieving ~400 GFLOPS effective? I'm confusing myself. Let me just PROFILE to find the hotspot. Let me use ncu or torch.profiler. Actually — the key realization: the dequant overhead (sub+mul per element, in fp32) is significant. Each int4 element requires: unpack (shift/mask), subtract zero, multiply scale, then multiply x, add to acc. That's ~5-6 ops per element. The MAC part (mul+add) is what we want; the dequant (unpack+sub+mul) is overhead. For a W4A16 GEMV, the dequant overhead is inherent. But it makes it compute-heavier than a pure bf16 GEMV. To go faster, I'd need tensor cores (but M=1 GEMV can't use them efficiently) OR reduce dequant overhead. Hmm. Actually — the REAL question: is my kernel compute-bound or bandwidth-bound? Let me profile with ncu to get the achieved bandwidth and compute utilization. Let me run ncu on the kernel. Actually, let me reconsider the numbers. 4.5ms. If I look at JUST the MoE (the bulk, ~136MB weight, 9 experts × 4 blocks): 9 experts × (gate+up+down) × 2.36M elements = 9*3*2.36M = 64M elements per block × 4 blocks = 255M elements. Plus the dequant. Let me just profile. Let me use ncu (the wrapper). ncu needs the lock too but it's a profiling tool. Let me run ncu on a single step. Actually — given time constraints and the GPU contention, let me reconsider the strategy. The geomean is 1.18x. To improve, I need to reduce the per-token time, especially at long context (MLA). The MLA at long context: scores + v_accum are O(L). At L=16384, these dominate (20ms vs 4.5ms at L=2048, so ~16ms is MLA O(L)). Wait — that's a HUGE MLA cost. 16ms for MLA at L=16384. The MLA does: scores (read c_kv 16MB), softmax, v_accum (read c_kv 16MB + p), o. The c_kv reads: scores reads 16MB, v_accum reads 16MB. Total 32MB. At 2TB/s = 16µs. But it's taking ~16ms! So MLA is 1000x slower than bandwidth-bound. So the MLA phases are TERRIBLY slow. Why? Let me reconsider v_accum: 16 blocks, each loops L=16384, inner 1024 outputs. That's 16 × 16384 × 1024 / 256 = lots. And scores: warp-per-l. Actually — the v_accum inner loop: `for(l=0;l<=L;l++)` × `for(out=tid;out<H*IW;out+=THREADS)`. For each l, 256 threads do H*IW=1024 outputs (4 each). Each output: 1 FMA (vacc += p[h]*ckl[il]). So per l: 1024 FMAs. × L=16385 l's = 16.8M FMAs per block. × 16 blocks. But only 16 blocks (of 228) work! The other 212 blocks idle (spin at barrier). So the phase time = 1 block's 16.8M FMAs. At 256 threads × 1 FMA/cycle × 1.5GHz = 384 GFLOPS/block → 16.8M/384G = 44µs. Not 16ms. Hmm. So v_accum ~44µs. scores: warp-per-l, L=16385 outputs, each 512+64 FMAs. 16385 × 576 = 9.4M FMAs / (228 blocks × 8 warps) = 9.4M/1824 = 5.2K FMAs/warp. Fast. ~µs. So MLA shouldn't be 16ms. Let me reconsider the measurement. ctx=2048: 4.5ms. ctx=16384: 20.4ms. Δ = 16ms for ΔL=14336. So ~1.1µs per token. What scales with L and takes 1.1µs/token? - scores: reads c_kv (L*512*2 bytes = L KB). Per token, 1KB. 1.1µs/KB = ~1 GB/s. The MLA adds (4.51-2.77)=1.74ms going from ctx=512 to 2048 (ΔL=1536). So MLA is ~1.1µs/token. And the fixed cost (KDA+MoE) is 2.77ms. So the FIXED cost (2.77ms at ctx=512) is the dominant issue, not MLA. 2.77ms for the KDA (3 blocks) + MoE (4 blocks) + MLA (1 block, tiny ctx). 2.77ms for ~226MB weight... but at ctx=512 MLA is small, so ~220MB weight. 2.77ms = 80 GB/s. Still way below 2TB/s. So the GEMV phases are at ~80 GB/s. Why? Let me reconsider. The barrier is the prime suspect now. 30 barriers. If each barrier is ~80µs, that's 2.4ms ≈ the 2.77ms! Let me test: reduce barriers or barrier cost. Let me try the __nanosleep spin AND reduce G. Actually, let me FIRST test if reducing G helps (quick). Let me cap G=numSM. Actually — let me reconsider the barrier atomicAdd serialization. 228 blocks atomicAdd to cnt[phase]. atomicAdd on the SAME address serializes. 228 × (L2 round trip ~100ns) = 22µs per barrier JUST for the atomicAdds. Plus the spin. So ~25µs/barrier × 30 = 750µs. Plus spin latency. Hmm, ~1ms. Not 2.4ms. But WAIT — the spin. After atomicAdd, thread 0 spins `while(*c < want)`. The volatile load. 228 thread-0s spinning. The LAST block to atomicAdd makes *c reach want. But the spinners don't KNOW until they re-load *c. The volatile re-load has L2 latency (~100ns). So after the last atomicAdd, the spinners take up to ~100ns to notice. Minor. But DURING the spin (while waiting for slow blocks), the 228 spinners each do a volatile load every ~cycle. That's 228 loads/cycle on the same cache line. L2 can serve ~1 load/cycle per cache line (or a few). So the spins serialize at L2 — 228 spinners × (1 load per ~100ns) = the cache line is hammered. This could SLOW the working blocks' L2 accesses (the GEMV reads go through L2). So the spin traffic STARVES the useful traffic! That's likely it. The spinning blocks' volatile loads contend with the working blocks' weight reads at L2. With 228 blocks, many spinning in low-util phases. Wait — but in the GEMV phases (high parallelism), ALL 228 blocks work (no spinners). So the GEMV phases shouldn't have spin contention. Only the low-parallelism phases (recur, softmax, vaccum) have spinners. And those are ~50µs total. So spin contention is only during those ~50µs. Hmm. So during the GEMV phases (the bulk), all blocks work, no spinning. The GEMV should be fast. But it's 2.77ms for 220MB = 80 GB/s. Unless the GEMV itself is slow. Let me reconsider the GEMV at 80 GB/s. With 228 blocks × 8 warps = 1824 warps, each reading weight rows. The uint32 loads are 128B coalesced. Should be fast. Maybe the issue is the SHARED MEMORY reloads and bank conflicts. In a GEMV phase, load_fold writes sx[K] to shared (K up to 4096 → 16KB), then ALL warps read sx repeatedly. The shared mem bank conflicts on the uint32-style reads (xb[b*2], stride 8) cause 4-way conflicts → shared reads 4x slower. But shared mem is ~100TB/s peak, 4-way conflict → 25 TB/s effective. The weight reads from global at 80 GB/s are the bottleneck (global << shared). So shared conflicts shouldn't matter. Wait — 80 GB/s global. The weight is 220MB. To read at 80 GB/s takes 2.75ms. So the kernel is reading weight at 80 GB/s. Why only 80 GB/s when peak is 2TB/s? Maybe not enough outstanding memory requests. With 228 blocks × 256 threads, but occupancy 25% → effective ~512 threads/SM × 114 SM = 58K threads. Each issuing loads. For bandwidth, need ~enough outstanding loads to hide HBM latency (~400 cycles). With 58K threads each issuing a load every few cycles... should saturate. Unless the loads are SERIALIZED within each thread (dependency). In gemv_dot_v, the loop `for(j=lane; j<K8; j+=32)`: each iteration loads row32[j] (1 uint32 = 8 int4), processes them (8 FMAs), accumulates. The next iteration's load is INDEPENDENT (different j). So the compiler can pipeline loads across iterations. But the accumulation `acc += ...` is a serial dependency (8 FMAs per iteration, dependent on acc). Hmm, but acc is one value; the 8 FMAs per iteration are on acc (serial). So within an iteration, 8 dependent FMAs. Across iterations, acc carries. So it's a long serial FMA chain (K8/32 × 8 = K/... for K=2304, K8=288, 9 iterations × 8 = 72 FMAs deep). Plus the loads. The serial FMA chain (72 deep) at ~4 cycle latency = 288 cycles per output. For the GEMV, each warp does ~N/(G*8) outputs. For q_proj N=4096, G*8=1824 → ~2 outputs/warp → 2 × 288 = 576 cycles. Fast. Hmm. I keep computing fast numbers but measuring slow. There must be something pathological. Let me reconsider — maybe the issue is the `red` shared memory and bsum in load_fold. load_fold computes sumsq via bsum (block reduce). bsum uses `red` (volatile, smem+4096). For EACH gemv phase with norm fold, bsum runs. That's a reduction over K elements by 256 threads. For K=2304, each thread sums 9 elements, then block-reduce. Fast (~µs). OK I'm going to just try the most likely fixes and measure: 1. Reduce G to numSM (114) — fewer barriers, less atomic contention. 2. Use a better barrier (less spin traffic). Actually — let me reconsider the barrier ONE more time. The atomicAdd: `atomicAdd(c, 1u)`. 228 blocks. But here's the thing — ALL 228 thread-0s call atomicAdd nearly simultaneously. The L2 atomics unit serializes them. But ALSO — the spin: `while(*c < want)`. The FIRST blocks to arrive spin for a LONG time (until the last block arrives). During this spin, they hammer `*c`. The last block (slowest) does its atomicAdd, making *c=want. But the spinners must RE-READ *c to see want. Their next volatile load sees it. But if the spinners are hammering *c (read), and the last block does atomicAdd (write) — the write must propagate. With 228 readers, the cache line bounces. This is the "thundering herd" on the barrier counter. It can be slow. The fix: use a hierarchical barrier or __nanosleep backoff or a different scheme. Let me try __nanosleep in the spin. And reduce G. Let me make both changes and measure. Actually — a cleaner barrier: instead of all blocks spinning on ONE counter, use a sense-reversing barrier with a flag, and have blocks spin on a SEPARATE per-block flag set by a "leader". But that's complex. Simplest: add `__nanosleep(50)` in the spin loop to reduce traffic. Let me try. Let me ALSO reconsider — maybe the real issue is that 228 blocks is too many for the GPU to keep resident AND the kernel uses 84 regs → 2 blocks/SM, but 228 blocks / 114 SM = exactly 2/SM. So all resident. Good. But 2 blocks/SM = 512 threads/SM = 25% occupancy. For the GEMV (latency hiding), 25% might not be enough → bandwidth underutilized. Let me try REDUCING registers to increase occupancy. The kernel uses 84 regs. If I reduce to 64, I get 4 blocks/SM (50% occupancy). How to reduce regs? `__launch_bounds__(256, 4)` tells the compiler to target 4 blocks/SM → limits regs to 64. Let me add `__launch_bounds__(THREADS, 4)` to the mega kernel. This might force the compiler to use ≤64 regs (or spill). Could help occupancy → bandwidth. Let me try launch_bounds(256, 4) and measure. And the nanosleep barrier. Let me make these changes: 1. `__launch_bounds__(THREADS, 4)` on mega. 2. `__nanosleep` in gbar spin. 3. Maybe cap G. Let me do launch_bounds first (most likely to help bandwidth). Let me add it. Actually — `extern "C" __global__ void mega(...) __launch_bounds__(THREADS, 4)` — syntax. Let me add `__launch_bounds__(256, 4)` before the function. Let me edit the mega kernel declaration.

Per KDA: ~49M MACs. ×3 = 147M. Plus dequant: each MAC's weight needs dequant (unpack + sub + mul). So ~3x the MAC ops → ~440M ops for KDA. MoE block (×4): - router: HID×64 = 0.15M. - gate/up: 9 × 2 × HID×M = 18 × 2304×1024 = 42.4M MACs. - down: 9 × M×HID = 9 × 1024×2304 = 21.2M MACs. Per MoE: ~64M MACs. ×4 = 255M. The reference's approach (dequant to bf16 matrix then matmul) uses tensor cores for the matmul (efficient compute) but wastes bandwidth (materializes bf16). Process uint32 efficiently. ``` __device__ inline float gemv_dot_v(const float* x,const uint8_t* wq_row,const bf16* sc,const bf16* zr,int K,int lane){ const uint32_t* r=(const uint32_t*)wq_row; int ng=K/GROUP; float acc=0; for(int g=0; g<ng; g++){ float ss=b2f(sc[g]), zz=b2f(zr[g]); float zc=zz*ss; const uint32_t* rg = r + g*16; // 16 uint32 per group (128 k) const float* xg = x + g*128; for(int j=lane; j<16; j+=32){ // 16 uint32, 32 lanes → lane does 1 (or 0) uint32_t u = rg[j]; const float* xb = xg + 8*j; #pragma unroll for(int b=0;b<4;b++){ uint32_t by=(u>>(b<<3))&0xFFu; acc += xb[b*2]*(((float)(by&0xF))*ss - zc) + xb[b*2+1]*(((float)((by>>4)&0xF))*ss - zc); } } } return wsum(acc); } ``` Here, per group (16 uint32), 32 lanes each do 16/32 = 0.5 uint32 → some lanes do 1, some 0.      int mb=1; cudaOccupancyMaxActiveBlocksPerMultiprocessor(&mb,mega,THREADS,smem);

-    mega<<<G,THREADS,smem,at::cuda::getCurrentCUDAStream()>>>((void**)ptrs.data_ptr<int64_t>(),(int)L,(int)gen,(int)prime,(int)stop);

+    void* p=ptrs.data_ptr<int64_t>(); int iL=(int)L, igen=(int)gen, iprime=(int)prime, istop=(int)stop;
+    void* args[]={&p,&iL,&igen,&iprime,&istop};
+    cudaError_t e=cudaLaunchCooperativeKernel((void*)mega,dim3(G),dim3(THREADS),args,smem,at::cuda::getCurrentCUDAStream());
+    if(e!=cudaSuccess){ fprintf(stderr,"coop launch fail: %s grid=%d mb=%d\n",cudaGetErrorString(e),G,mb); }

+import sys; sys.path.insert(0, ".")
+import torch, reference as R, solution
+dev = torch.device("cuda:0")
+for ctx in [2048, 16384]:
+    cfg = R.build_config({"context_len": ctx, "n_experts": 64})
+    rm = R.Model(cfg).to(dev).eval(); sm = solution.Model(cfg).to(dev).eval()
+    sm.load_state_dict(rm.state_dict(), strict=True)
+    for stop in [3, 4]:
+        st = R.init_state(cfg, ctx, 7); h = R.init_token(cfg, 7)
+        sm._stop = stop
+        with torch.no_grad():
+            for _ in range(3): h, st = sm.step(h, st)
+        torch.cuda.synchronize()

+import sys; sys.path.insert(0, ".")
+import torch, reference as R, solution
+dev = torch.device("cuda:0")
+ctx = 16384
+cfg = R.build_config({"context_len": ctx, "n_experts": 64})
+rm = R.Model(cfg).to(dev).eval(); sm = solution.Model(cfg).to(dev).eval()
+sm.load_state_dict(rm.state_dict(), strict=True)
+prev = 0.0
+for stop in [3, 31, 32, 33, 4]:
+    st = R.init_state(cfg, ctx, 7); h = R.init_token(cfg, 7)
+    sm._stop = stop
+    with torch.no_grad():
+        for _ in range(3): h, st = sm.step(h, st)

And each thread loops L (16384) strided. That's the same strided access. But now ALL blocks do it (redundant!) — no, I want each block to do a CHUNK of l. Let me restructure: each block handles an l-range [l0, l1). For all 32 heads, compute partial max over l-range, atomicMax. ``` __device__ inline void mla_softmax_max(const float* scores, float* smax, int L, int bid, int G){ int tid=threadIdx.x; int Lp=L+1; // block handles l-range; threads handle (l within range, h) int per = (Lp + G - 1)/G; int l0=bid*per, l1=min(l0+per, Lp); for(int h=0; h<H; h++){ float mx=-1e30f; for(int l=l0+tid; l<l1; l+=THREADS) mx=fmaxf(mx, scores[l*H+h]); // reduce within block mx = bsum(mx, ...); // need red if(tid==0){ atomicMax((int*)&smax[h], __float_as_int(mx)); } } } ``` This has the h-loop (32) outer, l-inner. For each h, threads reduce over the block's l-range. atomicMax to smax[h]. Coalesced? scores[l*H+h] for fixed h, l varying in range → strided (stride H). Still strided per h. But now distributed across blocks (each does L/G l's). For L=16384, G=456, each block does ~36 l's. 36 l's × 32 heads = 1152 reads/block. Fast. The strided access is fine for small counts. Then phase 2 (sum + write p): ``` __device__ inline void mla_softmax_sum(const float* scores, const float* smax, float* ssum, float* p, int L, int bid, int G, ...){ int per=(Lp+G-1)/G; l0=bid*per; l1=min(l0+per,Lp); for(int h=0;h<H;h++){ float s=b2f... no, smax is float. float mx=smax[h]; float sm=0; for(int l=l0+tid;l<l1;l+=THREADS){ float e=__expf(scores[l*H+h]-mx); p[l*H+h]=e; sm+=e; } sm=bsum(sm,red); if(tid==0) atomicAdd(&ssum[h], sm); } } ``` Phase 3 (normalize): ``` __device__ inline void mla_softmax_norm(float* p, const float* ssum, int L, int bid, int G){ per...; for(h) for(l in range) p[l*H+h]/=ssum[h]; } ``` These 3 sub-phases with 2 gbars between (max→gbar→sum→gbar→norm).
